title: The Silent Hand
blurb: Fire burns in her hands, but revenge tastes sweeter than any Michelin star. Seoul’s most feared critic hides a secret—one that could topple the king of Korean fine dining. A single USB. A city of knives. A meal long overdue.
comparable: For fans of *The Chef’s Secret* by Trisha Ashley, *The Paris Review* by Bella Osborne, *Salt Fat Acid Heat* by Samin Nosrat (with teeth)

chapters.text:
Chapter 1

The late-night stragglers filled Haenyeo Kitchen like ghosts. Two line cooks moved in silence behind the counter, hands quick as tide receding. Min Ji-eun braced her weight against the cast-iron pot, forearm veins popping under a scuba-apron that had seen three years of broth splatter and salt crust. The office worker in the corner swirled his soju glass, eyes glazed over the busted neon sign that flickered *OPEN* like a dying breath.

Oil hissed in the pan. She turned the heat down with the burner control unit—no thermometer, never needed one. The charred crust at the bottom of the doenjang-jjigae would bloom into smoke and depth. Tonight, Seoul would taste ash.

One of the line cooks, a kid no older than twenty, wiped his forehead with the back of his sleeve. “Boss, the second batch of rice is ready. Should I hold it?”

Ji-eun didn’t look up. “Fire it. We serve when it’s ready. Not when the clock says so.”

The kid nodded, already scooping steaming rice into a metal bowl. The office worker tapped his phone screen, laughing at something on his feed. Ji-eun watched the broth rise. The scum floating on top wasn’t dirt—it was flavor, rising to be skimmed away.

A buzz echoed through the hanok’s thin walls. A diner at the counter had posted on social media. Ji-eun saw the glow of a phone screen, the blue-white light catching her unshaven jawline and calloused hands.

“Trend alert,” the office worker read aloud. “The next big thing is ‘ugly fine dining.’” He snickered. “Look at this place—all rustic charm and smoky bowls. Guess we’re not fancy enough for Seoul anymore.”

Ji-eun’s grip tightened on the spoon. The kid didn’t react. He just kept plating—seaweed on one side, tofu cube in the center, thin scallion threads in a starburst.

She ladled the doenjang-jjigae into a stone bowl already lined with short-grain rice. The smoke curled out in thick, lazy spirals. The office worker took a bite, eyes widening. “Damn,” he muttered. Ji-eun ignored him. She reached under the counter and pulled out the burner control unit, slipping it into her apron pocket. Always took it. Never left it behind.

The kid wiped his hands on his apron. “You think we’ll get reviewed tonight?”

Ji-eun slid the bowl toward the office worker. “Reviewers don’t eat here. They eat next door at Gogung, sipping micro-lavender teas while we scrub pots.”

The office worker slurped another spoonful, eyes half-lidded. “This is better than theirs.”

Ji-eun turned to the sink. “Keep eating, then. Orders are open till last call.”

The line cook hesitated. “Boss, what if someone important comes in? What if—”

“If someone important wanted humble soup,” Ji-eun interrupted, “they wouldn’t wait for a post on Instagram.” She wiped her hands on her apron, leaving streaks of soy and sesame oil. The scallions on the counter looked too bright. Too clean.

A gust of wind rattled the paper door. The neon sign outside buzzed again—another notification. Ji-eun didn’t look. She knew. Another diner, another photo, another caption: “Found the anti-restaurant in Gahoe-dong. Chef looks like she wrestled a shark and lost.”

She reached for the burner control unit again, this time to turn off the gas. The office worker finished his bowl, wiped his mouth, and tossed cash on the counter without counting. Ji-eun pocketed the bills without looking. The kid swept the floor, the bristles clicking against stone.

Outside, the hanok’s blue roof glowed under the streetlight. Ji-eun stared at her reflection in the pot’s surface—unpolished, unfiltered, unapologetic. The burner unit in her pocket felt heavy. Reliable.

She picked up a rag and wiped the pot’s rim. The charred crust beneath it would make tomorrow’s broth richer. Every burn was a promise.

The kid paused mid-sweep. “Boss…”

Ji-eun didn’t answer. She was listening to the silence between the hanok’s wooden beams, the hum of the city beyond, the quiet pulse of Seoul’s hungry pulse.

Then—

A flash.

A phone camera.

Ji-eun turned. The office worker was gone. The kid was bent over the broom. The burner unit sat heavy in her pocket.

And the hanok’s blue roof—her roof, her kingdom—now carried a new mark on the map of Seoul’s fine dining.

Chapter 2

The Seoul Food Labs kitchen smelled like ambition and polished chrome. Eight chefs in matching black coats moved like a single organism, plating miniature courses on slate slabs that gleamed under recessed lighting. Investors in tailored suits hovered at the far end, sipping sparkling water, clutching pitch decks. The air hummed with the low thrum of refrigeration and the quiet clink of silverware against ceramic.

Min Ji-eun stood in the doorway, apron stained with yesterday’s kimchi juice, scuba-pattern frayed at the hem. Her boots left faint scuff marks on the polished concrete. The hostess—a host, actually, in a sleek navy vest—blocked her path. “Private event,” he said, thumbing toward the exit.

Ji-eun didn’t move. “I work here.”

“Formerly.” The host adjusted a cufflink. “Today you don’t.”

She stepped past him, boots clicking. The brigade froze. Kim Seung-ho looked up from the pass, tongs hovering over a spoonful of saffron butter. His face went slack. Then his shoulders tensed, like a man bracing against a wave.

Yang Jin-wook stood at the center island, a black tablet in one hand, a silver spoon in the other. She didn’t see him until it was too late.

“You,” Yang said, voice cutting through the hum like a cleaver.

Ji-eun stopped two paces from the pass. The chefs edged back. Seung-ho’s fingers twitched. A drop of butter fell to the floor.

Yang set the tablet down. “What are you doing in my kitchen?”

Ji-eun crossed her arms. The scuba apron stretched over her belly. “Tasting.”

Investors murmured. One woman in a plum-colored blazer leaned toward her companion. “Isn’t that the one from Haenyeo?”

Yang’s jaw shifted. “Security.”

The host moved in. Ji-eun didn’t flinch. “You’re serving my doenjang tonight. Third course. I want to know if you fixed the salt.”

Seung-ho’s breath hitched. Yang’s fingers curled around the spoon. “Excuse me?”

“Five years ago, you fired me for stealing Gogung’s signature dish. But you never changed the recipe—just the plating.” Ji-eun nodded at the slate slab where Seung-ho had just arranged a tiny rectangle of white rice, topped with a paper-thin slice of radish, a dot of brown paste. “That’s my doenjang. Same fermentation ratio, same umami depth. You just added microgreens.”

Silence. The investors leaned in. Seung-ho’s cheeks darkened. Yang’s voice dropped. “Get her out.”

Ji-eun didn’t move. “Or what? Call the police? Explain to these people why their golden boy’s Paris menu tastes like plagiarism?”

A chef at the grill turned off the flame. The hissing stopped. Yang’s eyes flicked between Ji-eun and the investors. Then she smiled—a slow, thin thing. “Fine. You want to taste? Taste humiliation.”

She turned to the brigade. “Seung-ho, assign her to dish pit. Scrubbing, not tasting.” She raised her voice. “And wash that apron. We have standards.”

Laughter rippled through the room. Some nervous. Some cruel. Seung-ho swallowed, then nodded at two line cooks. One of them—a tall guy with a shaved head—stepped forward, gloved hands outstretched.

Ji-eun didn’t resist. She let them steer her toward the back, past the prep tables, past the walk-in freezer door. The cooler air hit her face. The stainless steel sink gleamed. A hose hung coiled above it.

Seung-ho followed, voice low. “Ji-eun—”

She turned. “Don’t. Just tell me where the scouring pads are.”

He hesitated. Then, quietly: “I didn’t know you’d come.”

“Of course you didn’t.” She rolled up her sleeves. “You never do.”

Seung-ho’s fingers tapped his thigh. “It wasn’t personal.”

Ji-eun laughed—soft, humorless. “You signed the complaint. You let them pin a USB drive on me. That was personal enough.”

The tall line cook handed her a green scouring pad. Ji-eun took it, dipped it into the sink. The water ran cloudy with yesterday’s debris.

Seung-ho stepped closer, voice dropping. “I had a family to feed. A career—”

“Spare me.” She scrubbed a plate, back turned. “You knew I never stole that recipe.”

“And you knew Master Han’s name carried weight.” His voice cracked. “You could’ve handled it differently.”

Ji-eun flipped the plate. Soap bubbles clung to the rim. “How? By asking nicely? While he was boning interns in the walk-in?” She glanced at him over her shoulder. “You’re a coward, Seung-ho. Not a traitor. Not yet.”

He flinched. The accusation hung between them, unchallenged. Seung-ho opened his mouth—then closed it. The walk-in freezer door behind him rattled slightly, as if the compressor had hiccuped.

Ji-eun went back to scrubbing. The sound of metal on porcelain filled the small space. Seung-ho finally turned and walked away, his shoes squeaking on the polished floor. The brigade returned to plating, shoulders relaxed now that the spectacle was over.

Ji-eun kept scrubbing. The water turned gray. Her hands pruned. She didn’t look up until the kitchen’s ambient noise sharpened again—Yang’s voice rising above the rest.

“Attention,” Yang called. “Tonight’s Paris Preview menu begins with a course I’m calling *Jungsiik No. 3*—a reinterpretation of Korea’s forgotten seasonal palate.”

Ji-eun didn’t move. But she felt the shift in the air. The chefs stilled. Even the host at the entrance paused.

Yang held up a small clay cup. “This is *dotori-muk*—acorn jelly layered with pine nut ash and fermented pear puree. A dish once served only in autumn, nearly lost to history.”

Ji-eun’s grip tightened on the scouring pad. The water ran cold through her fingers.

“Credit for this rediscovery goes to my team,” Yang continued. “Specifically, our late-night research sessions led by the brilliant—” she paused, smirking at the room “—anonymous hands who dare to dig where they don’t belong.”

Laughter. More investors chuckling.

Ji-eun set the plate down. She dried her hands on her apron, then reached into the pocket of her scuba apron. Pulled out a small USB drive shaped like a *gamasot* lid. Black ceramic, rough glaze, unassuming.

She slipped it into the pocket of her khakis.

Behind her, the walk-in freezer door creaked open. A shadow stretched across the tile.

Ji-eun didn’t turn. She started scrubbing again. The scouring pad scraped the plate, rhythm steady.

The shadow stayed.

Chapter 3

The walk-in freezer hummed. Cold air bit at Ji-eun’s wrists where her sleeves ended. She flexed her fingers, then reached into the pocket of her khakis. The USB tile was warm from her body heat. She turned it in her palm—black ceramic, rough glaze, shaped like a *gamasot* lid. Ugly. Unremarkable. Perfect.

She set it on the edge of the sink, then took a single ice cube from the tray. The tray’s metal groaned as she pried it loose. She tapped the cube against the side of the sink until it cracked in half. One half went down the drain, a quiet *plink*. The other she placed on the USB tile, just to watch the condensation bloom.

Footsteps outside. Not Seung-ho. He always shuffled.

Ji-eun didn’t turn. She lifted the tile, let the ice slide off into the pocket of her apron. The tile fit snug against her thigh, a secret weight.

The freezer door opened.

Not wide. Just enough for a shadow to spill in—a silhouette framed by the alley’s neon glow. Green from a nearby *pojangmacha* sign. Red from a burger joint across the alley. The colors bled into the silhouette, making it look bruised.

Seung-ho stepped inside. His breath fogged in the cold. He didn’t close the door.

Ji-eun kept her back to him. She ran the scouring pad under the faucet, squeezed out the water. “You going to keep the door open? Let the whole block see you lurking in freezers like a pervert?”

Seung-ho didn’t laugh. “I saw you slip it in your pocket.”

Ji-eun dried her hands on her apron. The damp fabric clung to her hips. She turned, slow. “Slip what?”

He stepped closer. The door swung shut behind him. The neon light disappeared. Only the freezer’s harsh fluorescent tubes remained. Shadows cut across Seung-ho’s face, deepening the lines around his mouth.

“The USB,” he said.

Ji-eun crossed her arms. “I scrub dishes here, Seung-ho. Not USBs.”

Seung-ho’s jaw worked. His fingers flexed at his sides. “Don’t play dumb. I saw you take it from the dish pit sink.”

Ji-eun tilted her head. “You mean the sink where you had two line cooks ‘assign me to dish pit’ like I was a recalcitrant intern? The sink where I scrubbed plates for twenty minutes while you stood there waiting for me to confess?” She shook her head. “You really think I’d hide anything in my apron pocket and not expect you to search me?”

Seung-ho’s face darkened. He didn’t move. “You’ve got a point.”

Ji-eun waited.

Seung-ho exhaled through his nose. He pulled a hand from his pocket. Held up a crumpled paper towel. Empty. “I didn’t search you. I didn’t need to.”

Ji-eun didn’t take the bait. She leaned against the sink. The cold metal bit through her apron.

Seung-ho dropped the paper towel. It fluttered to the floor. “Fine. You’ve got something. What is it?”

Ji-eun reached into her apron pocket. Not for the USB. For the ice cube tray. She pulled it out, dangled it between two fingers. “This. I stole your ice cube tray.”

Seung-ho stared.

Ji-eun pocketed the tray. “Joke. Relax.”

Seung-ho’s shoulders didn’t relax. “You’re impossible.”

“And you’re paranoid.” Ji-eun stepped past him, toward the door. “If you’re so worried about what I took, maybe you shouldn’t have left a recovery tool on Gogung’s server after you wiped my user account.”

Seung-ho’s breath stopped.

Ji-eun paused with her hand on the freezer door. The handle was cold under her palm. “You ever use *DiskDigger*, Seung-ho? Basic tool. Recovers deleted files even after a full wipe. Takes about three clicks.” She tilted her head. “You don’t even need root access if the file’s still in the trash bin.”

Seung-ho’s face went slack. “Shit.”

Ji-eun pushed the door open. Light flooded in. The alley stank of fried oil and cigarette smoke. A rat darted past Seung-ho’s shoe.

Ji-eun stepped out. The freezer light behind her clicked off. She didn’t look back.

Seung-ho followed. “Ji-eun, wait.”

She walked toward the back alley exit, boots crunching on grit. The *pojangmacha* sign buzzed overhead, casting green light in pulses.

Seung-ho caught up, grabbed her arm. Not hard. Just enough to stop her. “What did you recover?”

Ji-eun looked at his hand on her sleeve. He let go.

“Nothing,” she said. “Yet.”

Seung-ho’s chest rose and fell. “I didn’t leave any recovery tools. I made sure of it.”

Ji-eun nodded at his phone in his hand. The screen glowed faintly—*DiskDigger* still open in the background. She smirked. “You just opened it.”

Seung-ho’s fingers twitched. He locked the screen, shoved the phone in his pocket. “You’re bluffing.”

Ji-eun turned toward the exit. “Am I?”

Seung-ho grabbed her arm again. “You have to delete it.”

Ji-eun stopped. “Delete what?”

“Whatever you recovered.” His voice cracked. “Before Yang finds out.”

Ji-eun turned slowly. She looked at Seung-ho’s hand on her arm. Not angry. Just tired. “Or what, Seung-ho? You’ll call security? Tell them your former sous-chef is stealing ice cube trays and having existential freezer conversations?”

Seung-ho’s grip loosened. He pulled his hand back. “Ji-eun.”

She stepped closer. The alley narrowed between the buildings. The neon bled together into a murky glow. “You framed me five years ago. You let them ruin my name. You watched Yang turn my recipes into Parisian curiosities while you played along because you were scared.” Her voice dropped. “And now you’re scared again. But this time it’s *you* who might get caught in the lie.”

Seung-ho’s breath hitched. “I didn’t know about the recovery tool.”

“Didn’t know?” Ji-eun laughed. “You never know, Seung-ho. That’s the problem.”

A truck rumbled past the alley mouth, headlights slicing through the gap. For a second, Seung-ho’s face was lit in stark white. Then darkness swallowed him again.

Ji-eun didn’t move. The USB tile pressed against her thigh. The cold from the freezer still clung to her skin.

Seung-ho finally spoke, quieter now. “What do you want?”

Ji-eun stepped back. “I want what I’ve always wanted. Credit. A chance to cook my food without having to sneak into kitchens like a ghost.”

Seung-ho’s eyes flicked to the alley exit. “That’s not—”

“It’s not yours to give,” Ji-eun said. “But you can make it easier. Or harder.”

The silence stretched. The truck’s engine faded. A cicada buzzed somewhere in the *hanok* garden next door.

Seung-ho swallowed. “I’ll delete the tool.”

Ji-eun nodded. “Good.”

He didn’t move. Neither did she. The USB tile felt heavier.

Then, from the alley mouth, a voice. Smooth. Amused.

“Well. This is cozy.”

Ji-eun didn’t turn. But her muscles tightened.

Lee Dae-hyun stepped into the alley’s dim light. Hands in his pockets. Camera strap slung over one shoulder. He looked between them, smile lazy, eyes sharp.

“Did I interrupt a lovers’ spat?” he asked.

Seung-ho’s face flushed. “This isn’t—”

Ji-eun finally turned. “Dae-hyun.”

He tilted his head. “Min Ji-eun. In the wild. And with a man who looks like he’s about to faint in fear.”

Seung-ho’s hands curled into fists. Dae-hyun’s grin didn’t waver.

Ji-eun stepped between them. “What are you doing here?”

Dae-hyun shrugged. “Same as you. Snooping.” He nodded at the Gogung back door. “Saw you come in. Thought I’d see what the Steel Mama was up to.”

Seung-ho’s jaw clenched. “She’s not—”

Ji-eun cut him off. “You followed me?”

Dae-hyun’s smile flickered. Just for a second. “More like I’m tracking a story. Gogung’s Paris Preview. Yang’s big play.” He tilted his camera. “And you? You look like you’re about to steal state secrets from a freezer.”

Ji-eun didn’t answer. The USB tile dug into her thigh.

Seung-ho broke the silence. “She’s not stealing anything.”

Dae-hyun’s eyebrows rose. “Oh? Then what’s in your apron pocket, *Steel Mama*?”

Ji-eun didn’t look down. She didn’t move.

Dae-hyun stepped closer. Not toward her. Toward Seung-ho. “You two look like you’ve seen a ghost. Did you?”

Seung-ho’s breath came fast. “Stay out of this.”

Dae-hyun ignored him. His eyes stayed on Ji-eun. “You know, I’ve been shooting Yang’s Paris tests all week. Tasting dishes that don’t taste like anything new.” He tapped his temple. “I’ve got a nose for plagiarism. And yours? Not even close.”

The alley seemed to shrink. The neon buzzed. A moth fluttered past the light.

Ji-eun exhaled. Slow. Controlled. “Why are you telling me this?”

Dae-hyun’s smile softened. Just a little. “Because I hate being lied to. And I think you do too.”

Seung-ho shifted. His shoes scraped concrete. “Ji-eun, don’t—”

She stepped around him. Toward Dae-hyun. Close enough to smell the starch on his shirt, the faint scent of his cologne—something citrusy, expensive.

Dae-hyun didn’t flinch. “You want to show me what’s so heavy in your pocket?”

Ji-eun didn’t answer. She reached into her apron. Pulled out the USB tile.

Dae-hyun’s breath caught. Just a hitch. Then it smoothed out.

Ji-eun held it up. Let it dangle between them. “You ever see a *gamasot* lid used as a USB?”

Dae-hyun didn’t touch it. “No.”

“Neither have I,” Ji-eun said. “Until today.”

The tile gleamed under the faint neon. Dae-hyun’s fingers twitched at his sides. Seung-ho’s breath was a ragged thing in the dark.

Ji-eun pocketed the USB again. “You following me, Dae-hyun?”

“Maybe.”

“Why?”

He met her eyes. “Because I want to know what you know.”

Seung-ho’s voice cut in. “She doesn’t know anything.”

Dae-hyun didn’t look away from Ji-eun. “That’s not what the freezer suggested.”

Silence. Thick. Heavy.

Ji-eun finally broke it. “You want to make it worth my while?”

Dae-hyun’s grin returned. Slow. Knowing. “Always.”

The USB tile pressed against Ji-eun’s thigh. Her pulse thrummed in her fingertips.

The alley stretched between them, a divide neither had crossed yet.

Chapter 4

Yang Jin-wook pressed the champagne flute to her lips. The bubbles stung. Imported. Expensive. She swallowed, let the chill fill her mouth before she lowered the glass.

Across the glass-walled office, the investors shifted in their chairs. PR flack hovered near the door, clutching a tablet like a life preserver. The press had been fed, the champagne poured, the photos staged. Perfect light. Perfect smiles. Perfect lies.

Yang set the flute down. The rim left a faint smudge on the walnut desk.

One of the investors, a woman in a silk blouse the color of eggplant, leaned forward. “You’ll have to walk us through the Paris Preview Week again. The logistics sound… ambitious.”

Yang didn’t look at her. She kept her gaze on the skyline—Gangnam’s glass needles stabbing the sky, the Han River ribboned below. “Two weeks of private dinners for the Paris jury. First seating at Gogung Seoul, then Seoul Food Labs for the experimental courses. Third wave: pop-up in Insadong. Each menu highlights a different regional technique—*sanchae* fermentation, *hwangap* jerky, *jeongol* broths refined for French palates.” She tapped the desk. “No Korean name on the invite. Just Gogung. Global.”

The word hung. Global. Legacy. The kind of word that made investors lean in.

The eggplant-shirt woman scribbled. “And the critics?”

Yang’s smile didn’t waver. “Only the ones who matter.”

The door slid open. A waiter slipped in, tray balanced. More champagne. Glasses clinked. Yang waited until the door sealed shut behind him.

She lifted her flute again. “To the future.”

The investors raised theirs. The PR flack’s camera whirred. Glasses met glass. Liquid swirled. No one noticed the way Yang’s left eye twitched behind the smile.

Her phone buzzed against the desk. A single vibration. Encrypted alert.

Yang didn’t reach for it. Not yet.

She gestured to the investor files fanned across the desk. “Let’s discuss the Paris real estate scout. The eighth arrondissement has a—”

The phone buzzed again. Longer. Insistent.

Yang’s fingers curled. She exhaled through her nose, a slow steadying breath. The investors didn’t notice. They were too busy flipping pages, scribbling notes, lulled by the promise of return on investment.

Yang finally picked up the phone. The screen lit her face in cold blue. The alert was from *Jeong Ji*.

She tapped. The message loaded.

No text. Just a headline.

*Gogung’s Paris menu tastes like borrowed memories.*

Yang’s thumb hovered over the screen. The letters blurred. She blinked. Focused.

It couldn’t be.

She opened the app. *Jeong Ji*. The headline was real. The subhead read: *A blind tasting by Park Soo-jin.*

Yang’s stomach dropped.

Park Soo-jin. The critic. The only one whose praise Yang still feared.

And the review was live.

Yang’s thumb pressed the screen. The review loaded. Three short paragraphs. Bitter. Precise. Devastating.

*Each dish is a facsimile, a dish I have tasted before—in old Gogung menus, in Master Han’s classroom, in the private kitchen of a young chef no one remembers. The Paris adaptation isn’t innovation. It’s erasure.*

Yang’s jaw locked.

The investors were still talking. The PR flack was whispering into her headset. The world moved. The glass walls held the chaos outside at bay.

Yang’s thumb moved. She opened the comments section. Zero replies. The review was fresh. Less than a minute old.

She scrolled. No author name. Only the avatar—a stylized pen nib.

*Jeong Ji.*

Yang’s breath stopped.

The pen nib. The style. The voice. It was hers.

She hadn’t written this.

But it was hers.

Yang’s fingers tightened around the phone. The screen dimmed. Then brightened again. The review refreshed. No. Not refreshed. Updated.

A single line at the bottom.

*Min Ji-eun would have saved you from this.*

The words burned.

Yang’s chest locked. The investors’ voices faded into white noise. The skyline blurred.

She stood. The chair rolled back. It hit the glass wall with a soft *thud.* No one looked up.

Yang’s thumb moved. She opened the app’s settings. Blocked *Jeong Ji*. Blocked the pen nib avatar. Blocked the entire site.

She deleted the app. Twice. Emptied the trash.

The phone buzzed again. Another alert.

Yang didn’t check.

She slipped the phone into her pocket. The fabric clung to the shape of it. Heavy. Unwanted.

She turned to the room. Smiled. The investors finally noticed. They paused. Scissors in mid-air. Ink pens hovering.

Yang spoke. Her voice was calm. Measured.

“Change of plans. We move the Paris Preview Week forward.” She gestured to the PR flack. “Tomorrow. Invite the critics. All of them. Even the ones we weren’t sure about.”

The eggplant-shirt woman frowned. “Tomorrow? The venue—”

“Is ours.” Yang cut her off. “Seoul Food Labs. We open early. Full staff. Full menu.” She glanced at her watch. “Eight AM. Press breakfast. Then the first blind tasting by Park Soo-jin herself.”

A pause. The room held its breath.

Yang’s smile widened. Just a hair. “Get her here.”

The PR flack’s fingers flew over her tablet. “We’ll need to confirm—”

“Confirm it now.” Yang’s voice didn’t rise. Didn’t crack. It dropped. Became a knife edge. “Tell her the Gogung family requests her presence. Tell her the Paris menu is ready. Tell her it’s perfect.”

The PR flack swallowed. Nodded.

Yang looked past them. Out the glass wall. Toward the city. The neon signs flickered. The Han River glinted.

She thought of Park Soo-jin’s failing hands. The way the critic gripped the wine glass at their last dinner. The way she squinted at the menu. The way she laughed too loud to hide the stutter in her voice.

Yang’s fingers twitched. She wanted to crush the phone in her pocket. Smash the tablet under her heel. Burn the glass walls to cinders.

She didn’t.

Instead, she picked up her champagne flute. Drank the last of it in one swallow. The bubbles clawed up her nose. She set the glass down. The rim left a second smudge.

The PR flack had the door open. The waiter had cleared the tray. The investors had stopped mid-sentence, pens frozen.

Yang stepped toward the door. Her heels clicked on the walnut floor. The sound echoed. Sharp. Clear.

She paused in the doorway. Turned back. The glass walls reflected her, tiny and remote.

“And get Min Ji-eun,” she said. “Invite her too.”

The room froze.

The eggplant-shirt woman dropped her pen. It rolled under a chair.

Yang smiled. Wider now. Almost genuine.

“Tell her it’s time to cook for the world again.”

Chapter 5

The Moonlit Courtyard wasn’t a place for mistakes.

Ji-eun knew that before she pushed through the carved wooden door. The scent of aged oak and charcoal hit her first—then the heat, thick and deliberate, fed by a single cast-iron brazier glowing in the center of the room. Eight chairs circled it, spaced like pieces on a board. Three of them were already occupied.

Yang Jin-wook sat at the head, fingers steepled, sleeves rolled to the elbows. Two sous-chefs flanked her, arms crossed, faces tight with the kind of fear that didn’t need words. The third chair held an investor—slick suit, thinning hair, a glass of soju untouched on the low table. His eyes flicked to Ji-eun as she entered, then away, like she was a disturbance in a carefully arranged still life.

Ji-eun didn’t sit.

“You’re late,” Yang said.

“Not by choice.” Ji-eun stepped over the threshold, boots clicking on the packed dirt floor. “The alley behind Seoul Food Labs is a one-way street when the food trucks roll in. Someone hit my fender.”

Yang’s left eye twitched. Just once. The investor exhaled through his nose, a sound like a deflating tire.

One of the sous-chefs—a broad-shouldered man with a scar through his eyebrow—shifted his weight. “We’ve been trying to replicate her *sundubu*. It’s not working.”

Yang didn’t look at him. Her gaze stayed locked on Ji-eun. “And?”

“It collapses.” The sous-chef swallowed. “Every time. The custard breaks. The jelly—”

“Never happened when Min was here,” the third sous-chef blurted. He was younger, voice cracking just enough to betray his nerves. He clamped his mouth shut a second too late.

Ji-eun almost smiled. Almost.

Yang’s fingers tapped once against the table. The sound was loud in the silence. “You think she’s responsible for custard that hasn’t been made in this kitchen for five years?”

The investor finally spoke. “It’s a metaphor, Jin-wook. The custard isn’t the point. The instability is.”

Yang exhaled through her nose. A controlled burn. “Metaphors are for poets. We’re in a kitchen.”

Ji-eun stepped closer. The heat from the brazier licked at her calves. She ignored it. “You ever cook *sundubu* in copper, Yang Jin-wook?”

No answer.

“Five minutes at 68 degrees. Gentle heat. Or the proteins tighten. The gelatin locks too fast. You end up with soup, not custard.” Ji-eun tilted her head. “You didn’t adjust the flame. You just cranked the burner to maximum.”

The scarred sous-chef’s jaw clenched. “We followed the recipe.”

“Whose recipe?” Ji-eun asked.

No one spoke.

Ji-eun reached into the pocket of her apron. She pulled out a small silver spoon, the kind used for tasting. Rolled it between her fingers. “Master Han’s. From the original *sundubu-jjigae* he served at Gogung. The one he stole from my drafts, then fired me for leaking.”

The investor flinched. Yang’s fingers stilled.

Ji-eun tossed the spoon onto the table. It clattered against the wood. “You want to fix it? Lower the heat. Let the custard breathe.” She leaned in. “Or keep boiling it until the whole pot breaks. See what happens then.”

Yang didn’t touch the spoon. The investor stared at it like it might bite.

The younger sous-chef shifted again. “We tried lowering the heat. The temperature gauge malfunctioned. The system reset to default.”

Ji-eun’s eyebrows lifted. “Default sets to 93 degrees.”

Silence.

Yang’s left eye twitched, slower this time. Controlled. Dangerous.

Ji-eun straightened. “You know how to fix a broken temperature gauge?”

No one answered.

“Unplug it. Wait five minutes. Reboot.” She stepped back. “But then you’ll need to recalibrate. And you’ll need to recalculate the timing. Because copper’s thermal conductivity is different. It’s not stainless. It’s alive.”

The investor finally reached for his soju. He downed it in one swallow. Set the glass down too hard. “This is a waste of time. We should be—”

Yang cut him off with a look. “We’re not done.”

The investor’s hand hovered over the empty glass. He didn’t refill it.

Ji-eun watched them all—the panic in the sous-chefs’ shoulders, the investor’s white knuckles, Yang’s still face. The air smelled like copper now. Like failure.

Ji-eun turned to leave.

“Wait.”

The word wasn’t a request.

Ji-eun paused at the doorway. The carved panels pressed against her back.

“Park Soo-jin accepted the invitation,” Yang said. “She’ll be at Seoul Food Labs tomorrow. Eight AM.”

Ji-eun didn’t look back. “Good for her.”

“You’re invited too.”

The investor’s breath hitched.

Ji-eun finally turned. Half her face was in shadow, half lit by the brazier’s glow. “Why would I go?”

Yang’s smile was small. Precise. “Because I told her you cooked the Paris menu.”

Ji-eun’s fingers curled into her palms. The spoon on the table glinted.

“Tell her whatever you want,” Ji-eun said. “But you and I both know the truth.”

Yang didn’t blink. “And what’s that?”

Ji-eun smiled back. “That you’re out of your depth.”

She pushed through the door. The wood groaned. The heat stayed behind.

Outside, the Seoul night air was cool against her skin. She pulled her phone from her pocket. The screen lit up with a single missed call—unknown number. No voicemail. No text.

She swiped it away. Slipped the phone back into her apron.

A figure stepped out of the alley’s shadows. Lee Dae-hyun. Hands in his pockets. Camera bag slung over one shoulder. His face was half-lit by the glow of a neon sign advertising *dakgalbi*—bright red and pulsing.

Ji-eun stopped. “You followed me.”

“Had to.” His voice was low, rougher than she remembered. “Heard what you said in there. About the Paris menu.”

Ji-eun didn’t move. “You shouldn’t be here.”

“Neither should you.” He stepped closer. The scent of cigarette smoke clung to him. “Yang’s playing with fire. Park Soo-jin’s not the critic she used to be.”

Ji-eun’s jaw tightened. “I know.”

Dae-hyun tilted his head. “Do you?”

She almost walked away. Almost.

But then he pulled something from his bag. A small black device. A USB adapter. He held it out. “This was in the server at Seoul Food Labs. I didn’t know what it was until I ran a recovery scan.”

Ji-eun didn’t take it. “Where did you find it?”

“On the backup drive.” He tapped the adapter. “Seung-ho’s last save. Deleted. But not gone.”

Ji-eun’s breath stuttered. Just once.

Dae-hyun’s eyes flickered. “It’s full of your old drafts. Recipe versions. The ones from before the scandal.” He lowered his voice. “It proves you never stole. It proves Yang and Seung-ho framed you.”

Ji-eun stared at the adapter. At the tiny green light blinking on its side.

“Why now?” she asked.

“Because I saw the review.” His thumb rubbed the adapter’s edge. “*Jeong Ji*. The one that just dropped. I recognized the voice. I recognized the rhythm.” He swallowed. “It was you. Or someone who knew you better than you think.”

Ji-eun’s fingers twitched. “You don’t know what you’re holding.”

“I know what it’s not.” He stepped closer. Close enough that she could see the grain of his stubble. Hear the hitch in his breathing. “I know it’s not fake. I know it’s not stolen. I know you weren’t the one who lied five years ago.”

Ji-eun’s voice dropped. “And if I say you’re wrong?”

Dae-hyun didn’t flinch. “Then prove it.”

The adapter hung between them, suspended in the humid Seoul air.

Ji-eun reached out. Her fingers brushed his. Warm. Steady.

She took the adapter.

Turned it over in her palm.

Behind her, the door to The Moonlit Courtyard creaked open. A sliver of orange light spilled onto the alley’s pavement.

Ji-eun didn’t look back.

She slipped the adapter into her pocket. Next to her phone. Next to the spoon she’d taken from the table.

Dae-hyun’s eyes followed the motion. “What are you going to do with it?”

Ji-eun took a step backward. Into the shadows.

“Cook,” she said.

Then she was gone.

Chapter 6

The Seoul Food Labs building was always quiet at 2:17 AM. Not empty—never empty—but quiet. The hum of refrigeration units, the distant drip of a leaky faucet in the staff washroom, the occasional metallic groan of the old *hanok* wood settling under the weight of investor money. Ji-eun knew the rhythms. She’d mapped them during her five years at Gogung.

She crouched behind a stack of stainless-steel tubs labeled *Export Only: Paris Menu*, the same tubs she’d scrubbed raw every night for three years. The scent of sanitizer and fear clung to the plastic. She adjusted the strap of her cross-body bag. Inside, wrapped in a dish towel, nestled the cloned USB drive—smaller than a thumbnail, harmless-looking. Useless without the original. But she wasn’t here for the original.

She was here for the server.

A janitor shuffled past, pushing a squeaky mop bucket. He paused at the thermostat by the service elevator. Frowned. Muttered something about the damn air conditioning cutting out again. He tapped the panel. The screen flickered. He sighed, scratched his temple, and kept walking.

Ji-eun waited ten full seconds. Then she moved.

The maintenance closet was unlocked. Always unlocked. Yang’s paranoia didn’t extend to janitors. She slipped inside, closed the door behind her. The air smelled of bleach and dust. A single flickering fluorescent light cast long shadows over the pipes and circuit breakers.

She knelt. Pulled a small screwdriver from her bag. Not hers—borrowed from Haenyeo Kitchen, tucked into the knife roll where it wouldn’t be missed until lunch. She unscrewed the access panel on the server rack. The screws were stiff. Her fingers ached. She bit back a curse.

Inside, the server hummed. Red and green lights blinked like angry eyes. She pulled the cloned drive from her bag. Plugged it into the USB port on the front. Waited.

A progress bar appeared on her phone screen:

*Cloning in progress… 0%*

She exhaled. Checked her watch. 2:31 AM.

The door handle rattled.

Ji-eun froze. The cloned drive was half-inserted.

The handle turned again. More forcefully.

She pulled the screwdriver from the panel. Left it dangling. Stood.

The door pushed open.

A woman stood in the doorway. Mid-forties. Hair tied back in a messy bun. A red Gahoe-dong Food Labs apron stretched over a frame that had carried too many late nights. Her eyes flicked to Ji-eun. Recognition flickered. Confusion followed.

“You’re not the night guard,” the woman said.

Ji-eun didn’t answer. She tucked the phone into her pocket. The progress bar on the screen read 47%.

The woman stepped inside. Closed the door behind her. “Only maintenance staff are supposed to be in here after eleven.”

Ji-eun nodded. Once. “I know.”

The woman’s gaze dropped to the open server panel. Then to the dangling screwdriver. Her eyebrows lifted. “You’re Min Ji-eun.”

Ji-eun didn’t confirm. Didn’t deny.

The woman exhaled. Rubbed her temple. “I read about you. The Haenyeo Kitchen thing. The ugly fine dining. The *Jeong Ji* review.” She shook her head. “I didn’t put it together until now.”

Ji-eun stayed still. The server’s fan whined.

The woman finally spoke again. “You’re the one who took Gogung down.”

A beat of silence.

Ji-eun said, “I didn’t take anything down.”

The woman’s laugh was short. “You exposed Master Han. You exposed Yang. You exposed the whole damn system.” She stepped closer. “I’m Lee Hye-jin. I used to work at Gogung too.”

Ji-eun’s fingers twitched. The cloned drive was at 78%.

Lee Hye-jin looked at the server. Then at Ji-eun. “You’re not here to steal data.”

Ji-eun didn’t answer.

Lee crossed her arms. “You’re planting something.”

Ji-eun’s thumb brushed the edge of her phone in her pocket. The progress bar hit 92%.

Lee’s voice dropped. “What is it?”

Ji-eun unzipped her bag. Pulled out the dish towel. Unwrapped the cloned USB. Held it up. Small. Black. Unremarkable.

Lee stared at it. Then at Ji-eun. “You’re *Jeong Ji*.”

Ji-eun didn’t nod. Didn’t shake her head.

Lee exhaled. Rubbed her face. “Damn. I should’ve guessed.” She looked at the server again. Then at the door. “They’re going to know it was you.”

Ji-eun said, “They don’t know yet.”

Lee’s laugh was sharp. “They will. When the files hit the server logs. When the due diligence report shows a hidden appendix titled *Plagiarism dossier, Gogung Paris line*.”

Ji-eun pocketed the cloned drive. Zipped her bag. “Then we make sure they don’t look.”

Lee’s eyes narrowed. “What does that mean?”

Ji-eun stepped past her. Toward the door. “It means you go back to mopping. And I go back to Haenyeo Kitchen.”

Lee didn’t move. “You’re walking into a war, Ji-eun.”

Ji-eun paused at the door. Turned slightly. “I’ve been at war for five years.”

She pushed through the door. The corridor lights flickered. Her boots clicked against the linoleum.

Behind her, Lee called out. “Wait!”

Ji-eun kept walking.

“Your phone!” Lee’s voice echoed down the hall. “You forgot your phone!”

Ji-eun didn’t stop. She already had it in her pocket. Already checked the screen as she turned the corner.

*Cloning complete.*

She kept walking.

---

The alley behind Seoul Food Labs smelled of fried garlic and cigarette smoke. Ji-eun lit a cigarette she didn’t want. The ember glowed in the dark. She exhaled. Watched the smoke curl into the neon glow of the *dakgalbi* sign.

A shadow moved at the end of the alley.

She turned.

Lee Dae-hyun stepped into the dim light. Hands in his pockets. Camera bag slung over one shoulder. The same unshaven jaw, the same tired eyes. But different now. Sharper. Focused.

Ji-eun flicked ash from her cigarette. “You followed me.”

“Had to.” His voice was rough. “Heard what you did in there.”

Ji-eun didn’t look at him. “You shouldn’t be here.”

“Neither should you.” He stepped closer. The scent of coffee and old leather clung to him. “Stealing data from Seoul Food Labs is a felony. Especially for someone with your history.”

Ji-eun took another drag. “I didn’t steal.”

“You cloned it.”

“Same difference.”

Dae-hyun shook his head. “No. It’s not.” He pulled out his phone. Swiped the screen. Held it out.

Ji-eun didn’t take it. Didn’t look.

“It’s a news alert,” he said. “Gogung’s Paris preview got pulled from the Guide Rouge shortlist. The announcement goes live at 8 AM Seoul time.”

Ji-eun exhaled smoke. Watched it dissipate. “Good for them.”

Dae-hyun’s jaw tightened. “It’s not good for you, Ji-eun. Not yet.”

She flicked the cigarette butt into the gutter. Watched it hiss.

“Why are you here?” she asked.

Dae-hyun pocketed his phone. Stepped closer. Close enough that she could see the tension in his shoulders. The exhaustion in his eyes. “Because I saw the review. *Jeong Ji*. The one today. The one that eviscerated Gogung’s Paris menu.”

Ji-eun didn’t react.

“It was you,” he said. “Or someone who knew you better than you think.”

Ji-eun finally looked at him. Her eyes were flat. “Proof.”

Dae-hyun pulled out the adapter again. The same one from the night in the alley. The same one that proved her innocence. “I recovered the files. From the backup drive. Seung-ho’s last save. Deleted. But not gone.”

Ji-eun didn’t reach for it. “You should destroy that.”

“Why?” he asked. “So you can keep pretending you’re not the one who brought down Gogung? So you can keep hiding behind Haenyeo Kitchen and your anonymity?”

Ji-eun’s voice dropped. “Because it’s dangerous.”

“Dangerous how?”

“Because Yang Jin-wook doesn’t lose.” Ji-eun stepped back. “Because Park Soo-jin doesn’t forget. Because the investors don’t care about truth. They care about reputation.”

Dae-hyun’s eyes flickered. “And you do?”

Ji-eun didn’t answer.

Dae-hyun stepped closer. Close enough that she could see the flecks of gold in his dark eyes. Close enough that she could feel the heat radiating from him. “I know what you’re doing. I know you’re not just protecting yourself. You’re protecting *jungsiik*. The movement. The style. The damn philosophy.”

Ji-eun’s breath stuttered.

Dae-hyun pressed on. “You’re not Min Ji-eun the line cook. You’re Min Ji-eun the architect. The visionary. The one who taught Yang Jin-wook everything she knows.”

Ji-eun’s hands curled into fists.

Dae-hyun’s voice softened. “You don’t have to hide anymore.”

Ji-eun looked away. Down the alley. Toward the flickering neon. “I’m not hiding.”

“Then what are you doing?”

She didn’t answer.

Dae-hyun exhaled. Rubbed his face. “Because I’m not hiding either. I know what Yang asked me to do to your photos. I know what I became.” He stepped back. “I’m trying to make it right.”

Ji-eun finally looked at him. Her voice was quiet. “You can’t.”

Dae-hyun’s jaw clenched. “Because I’m too late?”

She didn’t answer.

He stared at her for a long moment. Then turned on his heel. Walked away without another word.

Ji-eun watched him go. Watched the camera bag bounce against his hip. Watched his shadow merge with the darkness.

She pulled out her phone. Checked the time.

3:12 AM.

Plenty of time before dawn.

She slipped the phone back into her pocket. Adjusted her bag. Started walking toward the subway.

Behind her, the neon sign flickered. The *dakgalbi* sign buzzed.

She kept walking.

---

At 5:47 AM, Yang Jin-wook’s laptop chimed. The auto-update finished. The screen refreshed.

A single message appeared in the center of the desktop:

*Your Paris dish is copyrighted. Signature matches 2014 draft.*

Chapter 7

The lunch rush at Haenyeo Kitchen never slowed this time of year. Fifteen minutes past noon and the dining room was already packed—tourists in padded jackets, Gahoe-dong locals who knew what they wanted, and the usual collection of critics and bloggers hunched over small white bowls of *sundubu jjigae*. The air smelled like seaweed broth and burnt butter.

Min Ji-eun wiped her forehead with the back of her wrist, leaving a streak of gochujang on her temple. She barked an order to the grill station without looking their way. “Double the scallops. Now.”

One of the line cooks—Ji-ho, a wiry kid with a metal piercing in his eyebrow—nodded and slammed the spatula against the griddle. The sound cracked through the kitchen. Ji-eun ignored it.

Across the room, a regular lifted a hand. She didn’t see him at first. His table was tucked into the corner near the *ondol* floor heater, the one with the chipped celadon tile that always made her wince. He was older, late sixties maybe, with a salt-and-pepper goatee and a faded navy sweater stretched tight over his belly. He’d been coming every Tuesday for three years. Always ordered the same thing: *kongnamul gukbap*, steamed tofu, and a cup of barley tea without ice.

His name was Mr. Sung. He was retired. Or so he claimed.

Ji-eun set a bowl of fresh sea urchin roe in front of a tourist who shrieked in delight and immediately texted someone. Then she turned toward Mr. Sung’s table.

He was already staring at her.

She didn’t smile. She never did. Just gave a quick nod.

“Where’s Min?” he asked before she could speak.

Ji-eun paused. The ladle in her hand dripped broth onto the floor. She didn’t wipe it up. “Right here.”

He shook his head. “No. *The* Min. The one who runs this place.”

Ji-eun set the ladle down. Slowly. “I run this place.”

Mr. Sung leaned forward. His knuckles whitened around the edge of the table. “You’re not her. You’re never her.”

The kitchen noise seemed to shrink. The sizzle of the grill receded. Ji-eun felt the pulse in her throat but kept her hands still.

“You know her?” she asked.

Mr. Sung exhaled through his nose. “Used to. Before you ruined her reputation.”

Ji-eun didn’t move.

“You stole from her,” he said. “Took her dishes. Ruined her name. She never came back.”

Ji-eun looked at the ladle on the floor. The broth had turned pale, cold.

Mr. Sung pushed a napkin across the table. It was crumpled, damp at the edges. In the center, written in shaky ballpoint:

*Park Soo-jin wants to meet. Moonlit Courtyard. Alone. Tonight, 8.*

No signature. No date. Just a phone number scrawled in the corner.

Ji-eun picked it up. The paper tore slightly as she smoothed it against her palm. The ink bled a little.

“Why?” she asked.

Mr. Sung finally met her eyes. “Because she’s losing. And she wants to taste the real thing before she forgets how.”

Ji-eun folded the napkin and tucked it into her apron pocket. She turned away before he could say more. Walked toward the kitchen without another word.

Behind her, Mr. Sung called out, “She knows what you did. What you *are*.”

Ji-eun didn’t look back.

---

The alley behind Haenyeo Kitchen smelled of fermented kimchi and damp concrete. Ji-eun lit a cigarette with a wooden match. The flare illuminated the cracked brick wall for half a second before she exhaled.

Lee Dae-hyun leaned against the wall ten feet away. He wasn’t smoking. Just standing there with his camera bag slung low, hands in his pockets, looking like he’d been waiting for hours.

Ji-eun flicked ash onto the wet pavement. “You’re early.”

“So are you.” His voice was rough. “I saw Mr. Sung give you that.”

Ji-eun didn’t answer. She took another drag.

Dae-hyun pushed off the wall. Took a step closer. “Park Soo-jin hasn’t written a review in six months. Not since the Gogung scandal. Everyone thinks she’s retired.”

Ji-eun exhaled smoke. Watched it curl into the cold air. “She’s not.”

“You know that how?”

Ji-eun didn’t look at him. “Because she invited me to a private dinner. Because she remembers my dishes from five years ago. Because she’s the only critic who ever mattered to Yang.”

Dae-hyun stepped in front of her. Forced her to look up. “You can’t trust her.”

Ji-eun met his gaze. Held it. “I never do.”

A gust of wind rattled the dumpster behind them. Dae-hyun flinched. Ji-eun didn’t.

“Why now?” he asked.

“Because Yang’s Paris menu just got pulled from the Guide Rouge.” She flicked the cigarette butt into the gutter. Watched it sizzle. “Because Park Soo-jin’s last review is coming out in two days. Because Yang’s investors are pulling funding. Because Park wants to taste *jungsiik* one more time before she forgets what it even is.”

Dae-hyun’s jaw tightened. “And you’re going to give it to her?”

Ji-eun pocketed her lighter. “I don’t have a choice.”

“You always have a choice.”

She shook her head. “Not when the alternative is letting Yang Jin-wook poison fine dining forever.”

Dae-hyun stared at her. Really stared. His eyes moved over her face, her posture, the tension in her shoulders. Then he laughed. Short. Bitter.

“You’re still hiding,” he said.

Ji-eun didn’t react.

“You’re not Min Ji-eun the line cook,” he said. “You’re Min Ji-eun the architect. The ghost. The *Jeong Ji*.”

She looked away. Down the alley. Toward the flickering neon of the *budae jjigae* stall.

Dae-hyun stepped closer. Close enough that she could smell the coffee on his breath. Close enough that she could feel the heat rising off his skin.

“You don’t have to do this alone,” he said.

Ji-eun’s breath hitched. Almost imperceptible.

Dae-hyun reached out. Almost touched her arm. Then stopped. Dropped his hand.

“You’ll be walking into a trap,” he said. “Park Soo-jin doesn’t just forget things. She invents others. She’ll see patterns that aren’t there. She’ll tear you apart.”

Ji-eun looked back at him. Her voice was quiet. “I know.”

Dae-hyun’s eyes darkened. “Then why go?”

Ji-eun adjusted her apron. Pulled the crumpled napkin from her pocket. Smoothed it against her palm.

“Because,” she said, “Park Soo-jin is the last critic who ever believed in *jungsiik*. And I’m not going to let Yang Jin-wook steal that from me again.”

Dae-hyun stared at her. Then he exhaled. Rubbed his face with both hands.

Ji-eun folded the napkin and put it back in her pocket. “You should go. Before someone sees you talking to me.”

He didn’t move. Just stood there. Exposed. Vulnerable.

Ji-eun turned and walked back toward the kitchen door. The metal clanged shut behind her.

Dae-hyun didn’t follow.

But he didn’t leave either.

---

In the back corner of Haenyeo Kitchen, Ji-ho was scrubbing a pot with aggressive strokes. The sponge made a wet, scraping sound against the metal.

Ji-eun stepped behind the counter. Grabbed an apron from the hook. Tied it on. Looped the strings tight.

Ji-ho didn’t look up. “Everything okay?”

Ji-eun picked up a knife. Tested the edge with her thumb. “No.”

Ji-ho finally glanced at her. Noticed the napkin in her apron pocket. “What’s that?”

Ji-eun didn’t answer. She tested the knife again. Sharper than she needed. Always sharper.

Ji-ho set the pot down. Water dripped onto the floor. “You’re not going to that dinner.”

Ji-eun met his eyes. “I have to.”

“Why?”

“Because Park Soo-jin is the only critic who ever mattered. And she’s going to write the last review of her career.”

Ji-ho wiped his hands on his apron. “And if she remembers too much? If she figures out it’s you?”

Ji-eun tested the knife one more time. “Then I’ll deal with it.”

Ji-ho shook his head. “You don’t know what she’s like now.”

Ji-eun set the knife down. Pulled out the napkin. Unfolded it on the counter between them. The phone number glared up at them.

Ji-ho’s eyes flicked to it. Back to her.

“She’s losing,” Ji-eun said. “And losing people don’t care about truth. They care about ghosts.”

Ji-ho didn’t respond.

Ji-eun folded the napkin again. Tucked it back into her pocket. Picked up the knife.

“Lock up when you’re done,” she said. She walked toward the service window.

Ji-ho watched her go. Watched the way she carried the knife. The way her shoulders didn’t slump. The way she never looked back.

He picked up the pot. Started scrubbing again.

The knife gleamed on the counter. Unused. For now.

Chapter 8

The Moonlit Courtyard was supposed to be empty by eight, but Park Soo-jin stood at the entrance anyway. The speakeasy’s red lacquer door was locked, the blackout curtains drawn tight. No light spilled onto the flagstones. No hum of conversation leaked through the walls. Just the distant thrum of Gahoe-dong’s evening traffic and the smell of grilled squid from the alley food stall next door.

She checked her watch. 8:07.

Park smoothed her hair with both hands—once, twice—then pressed her palms to her temples. The motion made her wince. She swallowed hard and knocked. Three sharp raps. Then two slow. The code.

Nothing.

She knocked again. Louder. More insistent.

The door didn’t budge.

Park exhaled through her nose. The smell of soju on her breath curled back into her mouth. Her stomach clenched. She hadn’t eaten since breakfast. Or was it yesterday? She couldn’t remember.

She pulled her phone from her coat pocket. The screen lit up with a cracked digit: 6%. She dialed the number from the napkin.

It rang once.

Twice.

Three times.

She was about to hang up when a voice crackled through.

“You’re late.”

Park froze. The voice wasn’t what she expected. It was rougher. Coarser. Like a line cook who’d spent a decade breathing fire.

She swallowed. “Ji-eun?”

Silence. Then a breath—exhaled, amused. “Who else would answer a burner phone?”

Park’s grip tightened on the phone. “You know it’s me.”

Another pause. Then, quieter: “I know the voice. The way you order *kongnamul gukbap*—extra broth, no tofu. The way you tear the radish into three strips, never two. The way you leave the spoon in the bowl when you’re done.”

Park’s fingers trembled. She steadied them against the doorframe.

“You remember,” she said.

“I remember everything that matters.” A pause. Then a quiet, deliberate insult: “Ms. Park.”

Park flinched. “Don’t.”

“Don’t what? Tell the truth? You called me here. You led me here. You’re the one who still believes in *jungsiik*—or so you said in your review of Gogung’s *sundubu*. ‘A dish that tastes of nostalgia and neglect.’ You wrote that. I remember.”

Park’s throat burned. “I was wrong.”

“No.” The voice hardened. “You were right.”

Park closed her eyes. The alcohol haze receded just enough to let the shame in. “I need to taste it.”

“Taste what?”

“The real thing.” Her voice cracked. She tried again. “Before I forget how it’s supposed to taste.”

A long silence. Then, the sound of a door opening. Not the Moonlit Courtyard’s door—somewhere else. The clatter of dishes. A radio playing old trot music.

Ji-eun’s voice returned, closer now. “You walked past my kitchen. Twice. You could’ve walked in. But you didn’t.”

Park opened her eyes. The alley stretched empty in both directions. No Ji-eun. No familiar face. Just shadows and the steady drip of a leaky pipe.

“I was afraid,” Park said.

“Of what?”

“Of remembering.”

Ji-eun didn’t answer right away. Park heard her moving—steps on a wooden floor, the scrape of a stool. Then a new sound: the hiss of a gas burner catching. The sizzle of oil.

“Come to Haenyeo Kitchen,” Ji-eun said. “Tonight. After closing.”

“It’s not on the list.”

“No one cares about the list anymore.”

Park swallowed. “And if I get lost again?”

A beat. Then: “You’ll find me. You always did.”

Park hung up.

The phone screen died. She shoved it into her pocket and turned toward the alley exit.

She didn’t see the figure standing ten feet away, leaning against the wall, camera bag at his feet.

Lee Dae-hyun watched her from the shadows. His face was half in light, half in dark. He didn’t move. Didn’t speak.

Park froze.

Dae-hyun pushed off the wall. Stepped into the dim glow of the alley’s single bulb.

“You shouldn’t be here,” he said.

Park folded her arms. “I had a reservation.”

“Not here.” He gestured toward the Moonlit Courtyard door. “There. That place is a relic. A trap.”

Park’s mouth twisted. “I know.”

Dae-hyun took two steps closer. The scent of coffee and old film rolled off him. “Then why come?”

Park looked at the speakeasy door. “Because I need to remember.”

“Remember what?”

She met his eyes. “That food isn’t just molecules. It’s memory. It’s identity. It’s *me*.”

Dae-hyun didn’t blink. “And what about her?”

Park exhaled. “She’s the only one who ever believed in that.”

Dae-hyun stepped between Park and the door. Blocked her path. “Ji-eun isn’t what you think.”

Park’s pulse jumped. “You don’t know what I think.”

“I do.” He held her gaze. “You think she’s the prodigy who burned her bridges. But she’s not. She’s the ghost who never left.”

Park’s breath hitched. “You’re protecting her.”

“No.” Dae-hyun kept his voice low. “I’m trying to protect you.”

Park broke eye contact. Looked down at her shoes. The red paint was chipped on the toe. She hadn’t noticed before.

“I’m too late for protection,” she murmured.

Dae-hyun didn’t argue. Just stepped aside. Not because he agreed. Because the stubborn set of her jaw told him she wouldn’t be dissuaded.

Park walked past him. Down the alley. Toward the street.

Dae-hyun called after her. “I’ll walk with you.”

She didn’t turn. “I know the way.”

“Not anymore.”

Park stopped at the mouth of the alley. The neon glow of Gahoe-dong spilled over her like a tide. She faced him.

“Why are you really here?” she asked.

Dae-hyun met her stare. “Because I’m the only one who knows what you’re walking into.”

Park’s lips parted. She almost asked. Almost pushed. But then her phone buzzed in her pocket. A notification from an unknown number:

*“Bring your palate. Leave your reputation.”*

She pulled the phone out. The screen went dark before she could read the rest.

Dae-hyun stepped forward. Close enough to brush her sleeve if he wanted. “Ji-eun’s kitchen isn’t the only one that remembers.”

Park looked at him. Really looked. The lines around his eyes. The way his fingers flexed at his sides.

“You were her photographer,” she said.

Dae-hyun didn’t deny it. “And her judge. And her jury. I framed her dishes to make them look amateur. For Gogung. For Yang.”

Park’s stomach twisted. “Why?”

He didn’t answer. Just stared at the phone in her hand. The cracked screen. The dying glow.

Park slipped the phone back into her pocket. “So you could make her disappear.”

Dae-hyun exhaled. “No. So I could make sure she never had to.”

Park turned away. Walked into the neon-lit street.

Dae-hyun didn’t follow. Just watched her go. Watched the way she moved—less certain than before, but still walking forward.

He pulled out his own phone. Typed a message:

*“She’s on her way. Don’t cook the ghost. Let her see.”*

Hit send.

The phone buzzed in response almost immediately:

*“She won’t like what’s left.”*

Dae-hyun pocketed the phone. Then he lit a cigarette. The flare of the match lit up his face. He took a long drag. Watched the ember glow.

Park Soo-jin was already gone. Lost in the crowd. Lost in the city.

Lost in her own mind.

Dae-hyun exhaled smoke. Then he followed. Not to protect her.

To watch what happened when the ghost finally met her judgment.

Chapter 9

The bell above Haenyeo Kitchen’s door jingled like a cash register at the wrong moment. Ji-eun wiped her hands on her apron and didn’t look up. The lunch rush had thinned to a single table of office workers stabbing at *kongnamul gukbap* with plastic spoons.

Seung-ho slid onto a stool by the pass. His chef’s coat was too clean, the collar stiff with starch. He held a check between two fingers like a tip from a bad dinner.

Ji-eun dropped a ladle into the *doenjang* pot. The clatter made the office workers jump.

Seung-ho didn’t flinch. “Five years of compound interest. Yang settled on three grand a month for twenty-four months. No strings except one dinner a week at Gogung before Paris.”

Ji-eun picked up a paring knife. Tested the edge against her thumb. “You telling me this is my cut?”

“Your share of the Paris launch revenue.” He slid the check across the counter. The paper curled at the edges. “For old times.”

Ji-eun didn’t touch it. The knife stayed in her hand, blade glinting under the fluorescent tube that buzzed like a dying insect.

“Old times,” she repeated. “When I was the one writing the tickets and you were the one eating my scraps?”

Seung-ho exhaled through his nose. A laugh that didn’t reach his eyes. “You always had a way with words, Ji-eun.”

She set the knife down. Wiped her thumb on her apron. A smear of scarlet pepper paste remained.

“Take it back,” she said.

“You don’t want it?”

“I don’t want your money. I don’t want your guilt. I don’t want your sloppy seconds.”

He tapped the check. “This isn’t guilt. It’s closure.”

“Closure?” Ji-eun scoffed. “You mean the same closure you gave me when you left the knife in the drawer and walked out?”

Seung-ho’s jaw tightened. His fingers twitched. “I protected us both.”

“Us both.” She leaned forward. “Funny. I don’t remember an us. I remember a you. Sneaking server logs to Yang. Deleting the backups you didn’t know I’d mirrored. Leaving my drafts on the public drive like they were trash.”

“It wasn’t personal.”

“It was professional.” She plucked the check from the counter. Held it over the *doenjang* pot. The steam curled up, hungry. “Now it’s personal.”

Seung-ho didn’t stop her. Didn’t lunge. Just watched the check flutter into the bubbling darkness. The soy paste swallowed it whole.

Ji-eun set the pot’s lid back in place. Turned to the sink. Ran hot water over her hands. The soap smelled like citrus and failure.

Seung-ho cleared his throat. “You could’ve cashed it.”

“Could’ve.” She dried her hands on her apron. “Would’ve made me complicit.”

“In your own redemption?”

Ji-eun turned. The fluorescent tube flickered. For a second she thought the bulb was dying. Then she realized it was Seung-ho’s grin flickering—nervous, cracking under the weight of his own words.

“Redemption isn’t what you think,” she said. “It’s not a check you write to yourself. It’s not a pot you dump your sins into and walk away.”

Seung-ho swallowed. His Adam’s apple bobbed like a cork in rough water. “Then what is it?”

Ji-eun reached under the counter. Came up with a folded note. Simple white rice paper. One character inked in black: 恕.

She held it out.

Seung-ho stared at it. Didn’t take it. “That’s it? One word?”

“One word.” She let the paper hover. “Means forgiveness. Or absence. Or both.”

He reached for it. She pulled it back just enough to make him stretch.

“What do you want from me, Ji-eun?”

“Nothing.”

“Then why give me this?”

“Because you need it more than I do.” She placed the note on the counter between them. “Take it. Eat it. Burn it. I don’t care.”

Seung-ho unfolded the paper. Studied the brushstroke. The ink was still wet at the edges.

“It’s—”

“Empty?” Ji-eun said. “Or full? Depends on the man holding it.”

He refolded it. Slid it into his front pocket. Next to his phone. Next to a half-empty pack of menthols.

“You’re still bitter,” he said.

Ji-eun picked up the knife again. Trimmed the wilted scallion tops with precision cuts. Green confetti rained onto the cutting board.

“Bitter’s how you describe a dish that’s sharp and bright,” she said. “I’m not bitter. I’m hungry.”

Seung-ho’s phone buzzed inside his pocket. He ignored it. Kept his eyes on her hands. On the knife. On the steady rhythm of her work.

“You always were,” he said.

Ji-eun didn’t respond. The silence grew like dough in a warm room.

Seung-ho finally pulled out his phone. Glanced at the screen. His face went pale.

Ji-eun noticed. Not because he paled, but because he froze. Muscles locked in place like a character in a cheap play.

“What’s wrong?” she asked.

He didn’t look up. Typed a reply with one thumb. The other hand gripped the phone so tight his knuckles whitened.

“The Paris menu’s locked early,” he said. “Yang moved the tasting to tonight. Private. No critics. Just investors and a single judge.”

Ji-eun wiped the blade clean. “Good for them. Less chance of bad reviews.”

Seung-ho finally met her eyes. His pupils were dilated, black swallowing the brown. “The judge is Park Soo-jin.”

The knife slipped from Ji-eun’s fingers. Clattered onto the cutting board. A thin line of green ran down the blade toward the handle.

“Park Soo-jin,” Ji-eun repeated.

Seung-ho nodded. “She asked for it.”

Ji-eun picked up the knife. Wiped the blade one more time. Deliberate. Careful. She set it down.

“Park Soo-jin is dying,” Ji-eun said. “She can’t taste anything anymore.”

“She tasted fine this afternoon.” Seung-ho pocketed the phone. “At Seoul Food Labs. She ordered the same dish three times. Said she wanted to remember.”

Ji-eun turned. Faced the stainless steel shelves. The stacked bowls. The *doenjang* pot steaming in the corner like a forgotten ghost.

“She’s using the tasting to kill me,” Ji-eun said.

“No.” Seung-ho stood. “She’s using it to save her own palate before it disappears.”

Ji-eun didn’t argue. Didn’t turn. Just reached for the lid of the *doenjang* pot.

Seung-ho took a step back. “Ji-eun?”

She lifted the lid. The soy paste gurgled. Dark. Alive.

He turned to leave. The bell jingled again as he pushed through the door.

Ji-eun set the lid back in place. The steam rose between them and the exit like a curtain of tears.

Seung-ho paused at the doorframe. Looked back.

Ji-eun said nothing. Just stared at the check residue dissolving in the pot.

He stepped outside. The door swung shut.

Ji-eun waited three full seconds. Then she moved fast. Lifted the counter hatch. Slid under. The floor was gritty with old rice. She knelt at the base of the spice rack.

Her fingers found the loose floorboard. Lifted it. Inside was a small ceramic tile from her grandmother’s kitchen. Hand-painted with blue waves.

She pried the tile up. Beneath it lay a USB drive wrapped in wax paper. No labels. No marks.

Ji-eun tucked it into her apron pocket. Then she stood. Walked to the back door. Stepped into the narrow alley behind the restaurant.

The alley smelled of wet cardboard and old fry oil. A single bulb flickered above a service door marked “No Exit.”

Ji-eun didn’t walk toward the street. She walked toward the corner where the dumpsters stood. Behind them, a rusted fire escape.

She looked up.

No one there.

Then a shadow shifted. A man crouched on the second landing. His camera bag rested on the metal grating. His face was half in light, half in shadow.

Lee Dae-hyun.

Ji-eun didn’t call out. Didn’t wave. Just stood beneath him.

Dae-hyun dropped a memory card into her upturned palm. It was still warm from his camera.

“You framed my dishes,” she said.

He didn’t deny it. “To make them look like yours.”

“Amateurish.”

“Bad reviews.” He jumped down from the ladder. Landed light. “No one wants bad food, Ji-eun. Even if it’s honest.”

Ji-eun turned the card over. No label. Just fingerprints.

“What’s on it?”

“Paris test shots.” He wiped his palm on his jeans. “Raw. Unedited. Yang’s team asked me to enhance them. Make the plating pop. I refused.”

Ji-eun slipped the card into her apron pocket next to the USB drive.

“Why?” she asked.

Dae-hyun stepped closer. Close enough that she smelled the coffee on his breath and the faint metallic tang of darkroom chemicals clinging to his sleeves.

“Because I remembered,” he said. “Your *galbi-jjim*. The way the soy seeped into the meat. The way the pears melted like snow. It tasted like a dish that had been loved into existence.”

Ji-eun’s fingers tightened around the card. Her knuckles whitened.

Dae-hyun held her gaze. “I was wrong to help them hurt you. I’m sorry.”

She exhaled through her nose. A single, sharp breath.

“Sorry doesn’t cook the rice,” she said.

He almost smiled. “No. But it might let me eat at your table again.”

Ji-eun looked at the fire escape. The door at the top stood slightly ajar. A sliver of light cut across the alley wall.

“We lock the door at nine,” she said. “After the second seating.”

Dae-hyun didn’t move. Didn’t push. Just nodded.

Ji-eun turned toward the back door.

Dae-hyun called after her.

“Ji-eun.”

She paused. Didn’t face him.

“Park Soo-jin isn’t just the judge,” he said. “She’s the jury. And the executioner if she recognizes the ghost in your dishes.”

Ji-eun’s shoulders rose. Fell. Once.

Then she pushed through the back door. The light swallowed her whole.

Dae-hyun stood alone in the alley. The memory card burned a hole in his pocket. The camera bag felt heavier now. Like it carried more than film.

He lit a cigarette. The match flare illuminated his face for half a second. Enough for Ji-eun to see if she looked back.

She didn’t.

Chapter 10

Ji-eun’s studio smelled of burnt rice and printer ink. The fluorescent tube above the desk flickered like a dying firefly, casting jagged shadows across the wall. Hand-drawn flowcharts connected every Gogung dish to its *Jeong Ji* review, red strings crisscrossing like synaptic maps of revenge. A burner phone vibrated on the desk, its screen lighting up in pulses of blue.

She picked it up. Unknown number. Jeju dialect, slurred.

“You still there?” Park Soo-jin’s voice crackled through the bad connection. “That recipe on your desk—ghost recipe, tastes like memory but not mine. Whose is it?”

Ji-eun didn’t answer. Finger hovered over the *end call* button.

“Ghost recipe,” Soo-jin repeated. “Like the one I had in ‘92. The one I buried when the dementia started chewing at my tongue. You ever taste a ghost, Ji-eun?”

Ji-eun placed the burner on the desk. Turned it facedown. The screen kept pulsing.

A knock at the studio door.

Ji-eun didn’t move. Her knife lay on the cutting board, blade still flecked with yesterday’s soy marinade. The wall clock ticked in uneven jerks—battery low.

Another knock. Harder. Rhythmic. Someone used their knuckles like a metronome.

She stood. Walked across the studio. The floorboards groaned under her weight like old men arguing. At the door, she pressed her palm flat against the wood. Felt the vibration of the next knock before it landed.

“Open up,” Lee Dae-hyun said. “It’s ten degrees out here and I left my jacket in your alley.”

Ji-eun didn’t speak. Just slid the deadbolt.

Dae-hyun stepped inside, camera bag slung over one shoulder, a memory card pinched between his fingers like a business card. His breath fogged in the cold studio air. He wore a navy peacoat, the collar damp from melted snow.

“You don’t look surprised,” he said.

Ji-eun closed the door behind him. The lock clicked. The studio fell into deeper silence.

“You never are.” She snatched the memory card from his fingers. Not a transaction—an inspection. She turned it over. No label. Just her own thumbprint in the center.

Dae-hyun pulled off his gloves. Rubbed his palms together. “Raw files. Paris test shots. Gogung’s new menu, unfiltered.”

Ji-eun held the card up to the flickering light. Studied the faint green tint—chlorophyll from the garnish, subtle color grading he hadn’t bothered to remove.

“You edited them,” she said.

“No.” He shook his head. “They asked. I refused. Said the dish looked incomplete. Like it was missing a lover.”

Ji-eun set the card on the desk. Next to the burner phone. The screen pulsed again, casting erratic light.

“You came here to give me this?” she asked.

Dae-hyun stepped closer. Close enough that she caught the scent of roasted barley tea and the metallic tang of the camera’s shutter springs still clinging to his sleeves.

“To give you a chance,” he said. “To see what they’re really serving.”

Ji-eun tapped the card with one fingernail. The sound was sharp, like a nail on bone.

“You know what’s on it?” she asked.

“Not all of it.” He rubbed the back of his neck. A nervous habit he’d had since culinary school. “But I saw the *galbi-jjim*. Same recipe as your journal. Same technique. Same love.”

Ji-eun’s fingers curled into a fist. The memory card bit into her palm. She didn’t flinch.

“You think I’ll thank you?” she asked.

“No.” Dae-hyun didn’t look at her. His gaze tracked the wall charts, the red threads, the photos of Seung-ho sneaking into Seoul Food Labs at 3 a.m. “I think you’ll use it.”

Ji-eun picked up the burner phone. Thumbed the screen to silence it. Park Soo-jin’s voice cut off mid-sentence.

“You stole my work,” Ji-eun said. “Twice. Once in the photos. Once in the lies you sold to Yang.”

Dae-hyun exhaled through his nose. Sounded like a laugh that had forgotten how to laugh.

“I did,” he said. “And I’m sorry for both.”

Ji-eun set the phone down. Slow. Methodical. Like placing a dish on a counter for inspection.

“Sorry doesn’t heat the stove,” she said.

Dae-hyun stepped back. Put distance between them. The studio felt smaller now, the walls leaning in like critics at a tasting.

“Then let me cook,” he said. “Tonight. At Haenyeo. Just me and you. No cameras. No reviews. No revenge.”

Ji-eun turned. Faced the desk. The wall charts stared back like an audience. She picked up a red marker. Drew a single line through the thread connecting Gogung’s *galbi-jjim* to its review.

“You want to cook?” she asked.

Dae-hyun nodded. “I want to feed you.”

Ji-eun capped the marker. Placed it on the desk with a click.

“Feed me what?” she asked.

He met her eyes. His were darker than the studio’s deepest shadows.

“Truth,” he said. “Raw. Unfiltered. Like I should’ve given it the first time.”

Ji-eun picked up the memory card. Held it between two fingers like a coin.

“You’re late,” she said.

Dae-hyun reached for his camera bag. Unzipped it halfway. Paused.

“I know,” he said.

The studio door rattled. Not from knocking this time. From something outside pressing against it—something heavy, something impatient.

Ji-eun’s hand stilled. She didn’t look toward the door.

Dae-hyun did. His eyes widened.

“Ji-eun,” he said.

She turned. Looked.

The doorknob twisted. Slow. Deliberate. Like fingers testing a patient’s pulse.

Dae-hyun set the camera bag on the floor. Stepped between Ji-eun and the door.

The knob stopped moving. Waited.

Ji-eun didn’t move either. Her breath came shallow. Her fingers tightened around the memory card until the plastic bit into her skin.

Dae-hyun’s voice dropped. “We’re not alone.”

Ji-eun didn’t answer. Didn’t nod. Didn’t breathe.

The doorknob twisted again. Faster this time.

Then stopped.

Chapter 11

The back room of Seoul Food Labs smelled of charred garlic and ambition. Yang Jin-wook stood with her spine pressed against the stainless steel prep table, her phone angled so the screen’s glare cut across her cheekbones. The review loaded slowly—a trickle of gray text bleeding into pixels.

Gogung Paris: borrowed flavors, borrowed time

She read it once. Twice. The third pass, her thumb hovered over the screen as if she could claw the words back into darkness.

Seung-ho sat at the far end of the table, laptop open, fingers paused over the keyboard. His reflection in the screen was a ghost—pale, trembling.

“It’s out,” she said. Not a question.

Seung-ho didn’t look up. “Not the full review. Just the teaser.”

Yang dropped the phone onto the table. The impact made a wet sound against the stainless—a punch that echoed in the empty space between them.

“Teaser is all anyone needs.” She folded her arms, the sleeves of her chef’s jacket riding up to reveal the faint scar on her wrist from the Paris Preview trials. “They’ll read that and assume the rest. That we’re scraping the bottom of borrowed recipe books. That we’re frauds.”

Seung-ho finally met her eyes. His usually steady hands had gone slack on the keys. “The investors are already pulling the second tranche. The Paris logistics team canceled the venue scouting. The whole week is being rebranded as a ‘soft launch.’”

Yang’s jaw tightened. “Soft launch. Like a euphemism for failure.”

She grabbed the phone. Swiped to her email. The subject line glared: *Gogung Paris—Crisis Protocol.* Below it, a single line from the Paris liaison: *Clients demanding alternative venues. Suggesting discreet retreat.*

Yang texted back. *Not an option.*

The reply came instantly. *Then what is your play, Chef?*

She tossed the phone aside. It slid across the table and stopped against Seung-ho’s wrist. He didn’t move.

“You knew this would happen,” she said.

Seung-ho exhaled through his nose. A sound like air leaving a tire. “I knew *Jeong Ji* existed. I knew she’d surface eventually.”

“And you did *nothing* to stop it.”

His fingers twitched. He closed the laptop with a snap. “There was no proof. No connection to us. She was a ghost. A whisper.”

Yang leaned in. Her voice dropped. “She’s not a ghost anymore. She’s a *review*.”

A knock at the door. Three sharp raps. The rhythm of a critic who didn’t wait for an invitation.

Yang turned. The door swung open before anyone answered.

Park Soo-jin stood in the doorway, wrapped in a charcoal cashmere coat that smelled of rain on old books. Her cane tapped the threshold once, twice—testing the floorboards like a blind woman reading braille.

“You know,” she said, stepping inside without being asked, “ghosts don’t knock. They walk through walls. Or doors that aren’t locked.”

Yang didn’t smile. “Critic Soo-jin. I wasn’t expecting you.”

Park’s gaze slid past her, landing on Seung-ho. Recognition flickered behind her eyes—quick, like a lighter flaring in the dark. Then it vanished.

“Neither was I,” Park said. She settled into a folding chair by the wall. Her gloved hand patted the seat once, as if inviting a ghost to sit beside her. “But the review found me first. And I never ignore a teaser that tastes like poison.”

Seung-ho stood. The chair scraped back. “I’ll get you tea.”

Park waved a hand. “No need. Tea is for hosts and liars. I’m neither.”

Yang watched Park’s gloved fingers tap the table. One. Two. Three. A rhythm that matched the knock at the door.

“You think I’m lying,” Yang said.

Park lifted her head. Her eyes, behind the lenses, were watery and pale—like stones pulled from a riverbed.

“I think you’re scared,” Park said. “And fear makes people say things they don’t mean.”

Yang’s fingers curled into fists. The kitchen light caught the ridges of her knuckles, turning them white.

“You haven’t read the full review,” Yang said.

Park reached into her coat. Not for her cane. For a manila envelope. She slid it across the table. It landed between the phone and the laptop.

Yang didn’t touch it.

Park nodded toward the envelope. “Open it.”

“Why?”

“Because I want you to see what’s coming.” Park settled back in the chair, her posture changing—less critic, more oracle. “And because I want you to know I didn’t write that line.”

Seung-ho hovered near the kettle. Yang ignored him.

She opened the envelope. A single sheet of paper. Printed on cream stock, the edges singed as if salvaged from a fire.

At the top: *Jeong Ji – Fine Taste*

Below it, a dish name in bold: *Gogung’s Paris-bound Galbi-jjim: A Reconstruction of Min Ji-eun’s 2014 Jungsiik Recipe*

The ingredients list was identical to Ji-eun’s private draft. The technique section quoted verbatim from her notes. Even the garnish specifications—thin pickled daikon in a star pattern—matched the photos in Ji-eun’s archives.

Yang’s breath hitched. Just once. A hiccup in her composure.

Park leaned forward. “The *Jeong Ji* files are being mirrored. Not just to one server. To three. And one of them is registered to a node in Busan—under the name *MJE*. Min Ji-eun’s initials.”
