Yang flipped the page. A photo. A screenshot from a culinary forum. The title read: *Min Ji-eun’s Lost Jungsiik: Rediscovered Drafts from the Ashes of Gogung.*

The comments section was alight.

*Seung-ho left this in the Seoul Food Labs recycle bin.*

*Who the hell is uploading old files from 2015?*

Yang’s eyes burned. She closed the envelope with a snap.

“You’re saying she’s behind this,” Yang said.

Park tapped the envelope with one gloved finger. “I’m saying she’s *winning*. And you’re running out of time before the Paris Preview.”

A beep from Yang’s phone. Another email. Subject: *Clients Pulling Out – Final.*

Yang silenced the alert. Her hands shook as she stowed the envelope in her coat pocket.

“What do you want?” she asked Park.

Park rose. Her cane tapped the floor once. A punctuation mark.

“I want you to remember,” she said. “That the critic you fear is the one who made you. And the chef you buried is the one who can bury you back.”

Yang didn’t move. Didn’t speak.

Park turned toward the door. Paused.

“Also,” she said over her shoulder, “I left my soju behind. In your office. The one labeled *1992*. Drink it. Or don’t. But don’t believe in ghosts unless you’re ready to face them.”

She left. The door swung shut behind her.

The room fell silent.

Seung-ho finally exhaled. “We need to delete the forum post.”

Yang didn’t look at him. “It’s too late.”

“Then we sue. We bury the uploader. We—”

Yang’s phone buzzed again. A push notification from the culinary forum app. A new reply.

She opened it.

Beneath the title *Min Ji-eun’s Lost Jungsiik*, a single line glowed like a neon sign in the dark:

Meet the author at the old *gamasot* tile in Gahoe-dong hanok alleys.

Chapter 12

The Moonlit Courtyard’s back alley smelled of fermented chili paste and crushed herbs. Damp cobblestones glistened under the single flickering bulb of a security light, its glow cutting jagged shadows across the ancient brick wall. Min Ji-eun stood with her back to it, arms loose at her sides, the stained *haenyeo* apron tied tight around her waist. Yang Jin-wook blocked the alley’s narrow mouth, her leather gloves snapping against each other like castanets.

“You sent the teaser,” Yang said.

Ji-eun didn’t answer. She pulled the apron over her head, slow and deliberate. The motion tossed a curl of steam from the nearby *gamasot* teahouse’s vent into the air, swirling between them like a silent witness.

The white chef’s jacket beneath was new. Crisp. Embroidered across the chest in deep indigo thread: *Jeong Ji*.

Yang’s gloved hands stilled.

“You’ve been hiding in plain sight,” Yang said. “Not just cooking. Reviewing. Destroying.”

Ji-eun folded the apron neatly, tucking it under her arm. The USB tile in the jacket’s lining pressed against her ribcage—a curved corner digging into soft flesh.

“You framed me for theft,” Ji-eun said. “I just returned the favor.”

Yang took one step forward. “With plagiarism accusations? With *jeongjiik*?”

“With proof.” Ji-eun touched the embroidered name. “Five years of reviews. Every stolen bite. Every borrowed technique. Every investor lie.”

Yang lunged.

The movement was a blur of chef’s jacket and leather. Her fingers closed around the jacket’s collar, yanking hard. The lining tore with a dry *crack*. The USB tile popped free, skittering across the cobblestones like a startled crab.

Ji-eun didn’t resist. She let the jacket slip from her shoulders, pooling at her feet. Beneath it, her hands were steady. Her eyes locked on Yang’s.

“You don’t get to touch what’s mine,” Ji-eun said.

Yang knelt, scraping at the cobblestones with her nails. The tile lay half-hidden between two stones, its ceramic edge chipped from the fall. She snatched it up, turning it over in her glove. The gold contacts inside glinted dully.

“This is encrypted,” Yang said. “You think I won’t crack it?”

Ji-eun crouched beside her, close enough to smell the garlic on her breath. “Try. But the mirror nodes already have it. And they’re faster than you.”

Yang flipped the tile. The sharp corner caught the alley light, flashing like a blade. She brought it down hard.

Ji-eun caught her wrist mid-strike.

The tile hit the stone between them. The impact fractured the ceramic. A dull *click* split the night air. Black dust sifted into the gutter where rainwater pooled in slow, oily swirls.

Ji-eun released Yang’s wrist. Her fingers stayed curled, trembling just enough to betray her.

Yang stared at the shattered pieces. Nothing came out of the cracks. No files. No proof. Just the hollow sound of failure.

Ji-eun stood. She didn’t look at the remains. “You should’ve read the full review.”

Yang’s voice was thin. “What review?”

“The one that matters.” Ji-eun stepped over the jacket, leaving it pooled like a discarded skin. “The one about *you*.”

Yang’s gloved hand clenched into a fist. “You don’t get to write about me.”

Ji-eun paused. Laundry fluttered from a clothesline above—white tablecloths from the dining room, snapping in the breeze like ghosts waving. She turned back.

“Park Soo-jin already did,” Ji-eun said. “And she called you a liar to your face.”

Yang’s mouth opened. Closed. The alley held its breath.

Ji-eun walked past her. The cobblestones didn’t crunch under her boots. She moved like she was already elsewhere.

Yang didn’t follow. She stood frozen, the broken tile in her hand, the USB fragments glinting in the gutter like scattered teeth.

The back door of the teahouse creaked open. Lee Dae-hyun stepped out, his camera bag slung over one shoulder. He saw Yang. Saw the jacket. Saw the shattered proof.

For a breath, neither moved. Then Dae-hyun’s eyes flicked to Yang’s face. His fingers tightened around his camera strap.

“You okay?” he asked.

Yang didn’t answer. She dropped the tile. It shattered further on impact, the pieces skittering into the sewer grate with a wet *plink*.

Dae-hyun stepped forward. “She got to you.”

Yang’s voice was ice. “She got to *everything*.”

Dae-hyun’s gaze flicked past her. To the jacket. To the alley. To the USB dust settling into the cracks.

Something shifted in his jaw. A muscle twitched.

Ji-eun was already gone, swallowed by the night between the *hanok* walls. The only trace was the faint scent of ginger and soy sauce lingering in the breeze.

Dae-hyun looked at Yang. Then at the empty alley.

“You want to find her?” he asked. “Or you want to stop her?”

Yang stared at the gap where Ji-eun had vanished. Her fingers curled into fists again.

Then she walked toward the teahouse’s light, stepping over the jacket without a glance. Her boots made sharp, decisive sounds on the cobblestones.

Dae-hyun followed. The camera bag bumped against his hip with every step.

The alley door swung shut behind them, leaving the shattered tile and the broken proof to the Gahoe-dong night.

Chapter 13

The teahouse corners were where Seoul’s critics went to forget their own names. Wooden frames leaned against plaster walls, holding watercolors of monochrome mountains that blurred together under the dim light of brass fixtures. Park Soo-jin sat straight-backed at the smallest table, her hands folded over a fine bone-china cup. The steam rising from it carried notes of omija and aged yuja—just enough to mask the scent of soju she kept in her sleeve.

Kim Seung-ho arrived three minutes late, coat damp from the autumn drizzle. He didn’t look at her right away. His attention snagged on the wall behind her: a single hanging scroll, ink-wash of a sparrow on a persimmon branch. He exhaled through his nose, a sound that might have been relief or resignation.

“You wanted to see me,” he said.

Park Soo-jin lifted the cup. The porcelain caught the light, sharp as a blade. “Sit.”

He did. He set a leather portfolio on the table between them, its buckles undone. Files, photos, a half-empty bottle of twenty-five-year soju. He didn’t touch it.

“You’re drinking alone again?” she asked.

“Memory doesn’t need company,” he said.

She smiled. It was the kind that didn’t reach her eyes, the kind that waited for him to realize she already knew the shape of his forgetting.

Park Soo-jin slid a napkin across the table. It was plain. No logo. No crease.

“Read it aloud,” she said.

Seung-ho picked it up. The edge curled under his thumb. He read:

“Gogung’s braised *galbi* is tender, but the sauce lacks depth—replaced with a shortcut that tasted of soy isolate and frustration. The *ssamjang* smelled of garlic but carried no funk of fermentation. A dish designed for Instagram, not memory.”

Park nodded. “You recognize the date.”

He did. It was the week after Min Ji-eun was fired.

“You called her talent unrefined,” Park said. “Said her flavors were loud, her plating amateur.”

Seung-ho’s throat clicked. He folded the napkin, placed it back on the table like evidence.

“You wrote that every dish that followed was cleaner, brighter, more thoughtful,” Park continued. “A rebirth. A redemption.”

He didn’t answer. The teahouse hummed—a kettle singing in the back, the murmur of a single server arranging cups on a tray.

“Then the next review,” Park said. “Your voice changed.”

Seung-ho’s fingers twitched toward the soju. He pulled back.

“You said the *haenyeo* broth lacked balance, served in a bowl that looked like a scuba vest.”

A half-laugh escaped him. “That was her color scheme.”

Park ignored it. “After that, you stopped mentioning her at all.”

The server approached. Park waved her off with a single finger raised. The server retreated without a word.

“You wanted her gone,” Park said. “And you got what you wanted.”

Seung-ho’s jaw tightened. “It wasn’t personal.”

“Not personal?” Park’s voice sharpened. “Then tell me why you covered for the theft. Why you let them pin a crime on an innocent woman.”

He flinched. Not at the words—they were expected—but at the way she said *innocent*. As if the guilt had long since dissolved and only the label remained.

Park leaned forward. The light caught the silver threading in her hair, barely visible.

“You remember her knife roll,” Park said. “Black carbon steel. The way she kept it in her locker like a priest keeps a rosary.”

Seung-ho’s breath hitched.

“You remember the *jungsiik* menu she designed,” Park said. “Five courses. Each dish built on seasonal memory—*dasima* broth steeped in dashi from her grandmother’s kitchen, *gosari namul* flash-seared with pine nut oil. You remember the dill.”

His hands were on the table now. Flat. White-knuckled.

“And you,” Park said, “signed off on the deletion. Wiped the draft. Gave them the keys to her life.”

A drop of condensation slid from Seung-ho’s cup onto the portfolio. He didn’t wipe it.

Park reached for the soju. Poured a thimbleful into a second cup. Slid it toward him.

“Drink,” she said.

“No.”

“You will.”

He looked at the cup. The liquid caught the light like liquid amber.

“You drank with her,” Park said. “Before. After service. Before the scandal. She told me so herself.”

The past tense hung. *Was.* *Said.*

Seung-ho picked up the cup. His hand trembled just enough to make the soju ripple.

“What do you want?” he asked.

Park took a sip. Set the cup down with precision.

“Every dish you ever praised. Every dish you ever condemned. In order. With dates.”

He started. Just once. A jolt through the spine.

“You’re ill,” she said. “But you remember food. You remember *her*. I want the sequence.”

He drank. Swallowed. Set the cup down with a click.

He spoke.

He listed them.

*Juk* porridge with pine nuts, May 12. Praised for its silken clarity. *Doenjang jjigae* with aged soy, June 3. Called balanced, profound. *Sundubu* with clams, July 15. “Briny and bright, a whisper of the sea.” Each dish. Each review. Each date. His voice grew steadier as the list unfolded, as if reciting a liturgy he’d once known by heart and only now remembered.

Park listened. Nodded at the right places. Made no notes. Her eyes never left his face.

When the catalog ended, the teahouse was quieter. The server had left. The kettle had stopped singing.

Park reached into her coat pocket. Pulled out a napkin. Wrote in careful hangul:

*Alzheimer’s*

Then beneath it, in smaller script:

*I remember what you stole from her.*

She slid it across the table.

Seung-ho stared at it. His pupils dilated. His breath came shallow.

Park folded her hands. Waited.

He lifted the napkin. His fingers were shaking.

He stood abruptly. The chair legs scraped wood.

Park didn’t look up.

He walked away without a word. The portfolio stayed on the table. The soju cup half-full. The napkin in his hand, crumpled now, as if he’d already tried to erase it.

Park Soo-jin lifted her cup again. The tea was cold. She drank it anyway.

The teahouse door opened. A draft curled in, carrying the scent of street food—pajeon, hot oil, the faint metallic tang of grilled mackerel.

Lee Dae-hyun stepped inside. He saw her. Nodded once.

Park set her cup down. Smiled the kind of smile that knew too much.

“You’re late,” she said.

Dae-hyun walked over. He didn’t sit. He placed a manila envelope on the table between them. Bulky. Sealed.

“She sent this,” he said. “Early morning courier.”

Park didn’t touch it.

“She’s at Haenyeo Kitchen,” Dae-hyun said. “Cooking a private supper. Six seats. Your name is on the list.”

Park looked at the envelope. Then at him.

“Tell her I’ll come,” Park said. “After I publish.”

Dae-hyun’s jaw tightened. “You’re not reviewing this one.”

“No,” Park said. “I’m remembering it.”

She reached for the envelope at last. Slid her thumbnail under the seal. Pulled out a photo.

It was a dish. Dark broth, gleaming surface, topped with a single pine nut.

*Haemul dasima jjigae.*

She knew the recipe. She knew the hand.

Park folded the photo carefully. Put it back in the envelope.

Dae-hyun watched her. He saw the way her fingers lingered on the paper. The way her eyes didn’t blink.

“She’s cooking for you,” he said.

Park Soo-jin stood. Drape her coat over her shoulders.

“No,” she said. “She’s cooking for *us*.”

She walked out. The teahouse door swung closed behind her. The envelope stayed on the table. The soju cup remained half-full. The server returned with a fresh pot of tea, poured it without a word.

Outside, the rain began again. Soft. Persistent.

Chapter 14

The Gogung courtyard smelled of charcoal and cedar oil, the kind used to clean the tiled floors between services. Twisted gnarled pines framed a stone path leading to the *madang*, where tables of polished black oak waited under lanterns of rice paper. Investors in tailored suits mingled with journalists clutching leather portfolios. No chef’s jacket in sight—only silk, wool, and the faint gleam of watch clasps.

Min Ji-eun stood at the edge of the courtyard, arms loose at her sides, her hands rough and unadorned. She wore the same faded apron from Haenyeo Kitchen, the scuba pattern faint under the sharp light of three LED klieg lamps trained on the small stage at the far end.

Yang Jin-wook emerged from the shadows of the *daecheong*, her heels clicking against stone. She wore all black, a single strand of pearls catching the light. Her face was calm, the way a predator stays still before the strike.

“You’re late,” Yang said.

Ji-eun looked at her watch. “Seven minutes.”

“For the press, that’s early.”

Ji-eun’s gaze flicked over the crowd. Park Soo-jin stood near the back, her posture rigid in a deep blue hanbok coat. Lee Dae-hyun hovered near the food station, camera slung around his neck, lens cap off. He fiddled with the focus ring, avoiding her eyes.

“They’re waiting for you to speak,” Yang said.

Ji-eun walked toward the stage. The crowd parted instinctively, sensing the space between them wasn’t just air—it was history.

She stepped onto the wooden platform. The microphone was already live. She adjusted its height without asking.

“You know why I’m here,” Ji-eun said.

Yang climbed the steps behind her. The audience exhaled in anticipation, a collective inhale of social media breath.

Ji-eun pulled a single sheet of paper from her apron pocket. Unfolded it. Held it up.

“This is an apology,” Ji-eun said. “Retraction of the plagiarism accusation made against Lee Min Ji, formerly of Gogung. It acknowledges the theft of her *jungsiik* concepts, the sabotage of her name, and the suppression of evidence.”

Silence settled like dust.

Yang’s fingers twitched. The pearls at her throat weren’t a necklace—they were a choker, the kind worn to signal control.

“You want me to sign this,” Yang said.

Ji-eun nodded. “Publicly. On camera. Before the investors.”

“And in return?”

“You walk away from the Paris Preview. No global expansion. No legacy built on stolen recipes.”

Yang laughed. It was a dry, brittle sound. “You think I’d trade my future for a piece of paper?”

Ji-eun didn’t flinch. “I think you’ve already traded your soul.”

The journalists leaned in. Cameras whirred. Dae-hyun raised his camera, framing both women in the same shot.

Yang pulled a Montblanc pen from her blazer. The gold tip glinted. She uncapped it with a snap that echoed across the courtyard.

She reached for the paper.

Ji-eun didn’t move.

Yang paused. “What?”

“One more thing,” Ji-eun said.

Yang’s knuckles whitened around the pen. “You don’t dictate terms here.”

“Not terms,” Ji-eun said. “Correction.”

She pulled a second sheet from her pocket. Thicker. Printed on Gogung letterhead. Ji-eun held it up, letting the wind catch the edge.

The title at the top read: *The Jungsiik Accord – Transfer of Intellectual Property and Creative Legacy.*

Yang’s breath stopped.

Ji-eun stepped closer. “You don’t just apologize. You surrender the movement. All rights. All credit. All public association. Forever.”

Yang’s lips parted. For a second, Ji-eun saw the girl she used to mentor—the one who cried after service when a guest criticized a dish she’d slaved over for months.

Then the mask snapped back.

“This is theft,” Yang said.

“No,” Ji-eun said. “This is restitution.”

She held out the second contract.

“Sign both.”

Yang stared at the papers. The courtyard held its breath.

Ji-eun waited.

The pen trembled in Yang’s hand. She pressed the tip to the first page—the apology—and signed, her strokes sharp and final.

She tossed the pen onto the table.

Ji-eun picked it up. Smoothed the ink. Flipped the page.

Yang’s eyes burned.

Ji-eun uncapped the pen again.

“Wait,” Yang said.

Ji-eun paused.

“You said *correction*,” Yang said. “What correction?”

Ji-eun tapped the second page with the pen tip. “I’ve added a clause. Gogung’s Paris concept is no longer branded as *jungsiik*. The movement reverts to its originator. To me.”

“No,” Yang said. “Absolutely not.”

Ji-eun shrugged. “Then the apology is void. The plagiarism accusation remains in force. The investors will see your Paris menu—same dishes, same concepts, same fraud. Michelin will see it too.”

Yang’s voice dropped to a whisper only Ji-eun could hear. “You wouldn’t.”

Ji-eun met her gaze. “Try me.”

Yang’s jaw clenched. She reached for the second contract, pen hovering.

Ji-eun pushed the first sheet aside. “Sign the second first.”

Yang’s hand shook. She signed the transfer of legacy with a slash so violent the pen tore the paper.

Ji-eun didn’t look at the signatures. She folded both contracts neatly, tucked them into her apron pocket.

Yang’s chest rose and fell like a trapped animal’s.

Ji-eun stepped back. The stage, the courtyard, the investors—they all came back into focus.

She looked at Yang. Not with triumph. Not with pity.

With finality.

“One last thing,” Ji-eun said.

Yang’s fingers curled into fists.

Ji-eun reached into her apron again. This time she held up a pen.

Not the elegant Montblanc. A simple BIC with a chewed cap.

“This is yours,” Ji-eun said. “Left on the server console the night they framed me. I kept it. Just in case.”

Yang’s face went slack.

Ji-eun tossed the pen onto the table between them. It bounced once, rolled toward the edge, stopped.

The courtyard was silent.

Ji-eun stepped off the stage.

Yang didn’t move. Didn’t speak. Didn’t breathe.

Ji-eun walked toward Dae-hyun. He lowered the camera, his face a mix of awe and something raw.

She stopped in front of him. Close enough to feel the heat of the camera lens.

“You coming tonight?” Ji-eun asked.

Dae-hyun swallowed. “To Haenyeo Kitchen?”

She nodded.

He looked at Yang, still standing frozen on the stage, then back at Ji-eun.

“I’ll be there,” he said.

Ji-eun walked away. The crowd parted like reeds in a stream. The lanterns swayed in the night breeze.

She didn’t look back.

Chapter 15

The scents of aged oak and simmering soy tickled Min Ji-eun’s nose before she even pushed through the blue-painted door of Haenyeo Kitchen. Inside, the converted *hanok* exhaled warmth through the ondol floors. The dining room was half-empty—critics hadn’t arrived yet, but the line cooks were already in motion, sleeves rolled, knives flashing under the brass pendant lights.

Min tugged her chef’s coat straight, fingers brushing the folded contracts in her apron pocket. One more signature tonight. One more public stamp on the past.

A shadow moved in the corner. Lee Dae-hyun stood by the service window, arms crossed, camera dangling from his wrist. He’d changed since the courtyard—no pressed shirt, no uptight posture. Just a black T-shirt, sleeves pushed to his elbows, scuffed boots. He looked like he’d rolled out of bed and come straight here.

“You’re early,” she said.

He didn’t smile. “So are you.”

She ignored the jab and stepped past him toward the pass. The line cooks glanced at her, then away. No one spoke. The silence wasn’t reverent. It was wary.

Dae-hyun followed.

“You really opened a second floor?” he asked.

Min gestured to the narrow staircase behind the host stand. “Eight seats up top. Each table gets a different seasonal broth. Tonight, it’s black cod in five-year *doenjang*.”

He exhaled through his nose. “And the critics?”

“Already upstairs. Streaming live.”

He looked toward the stairs. Hesitated. “You’re serving it again, aren’t you?”

Min pulled a folded menu from her pocket. Unfolded it. The same dish. The same *doenjang-jjigae* from Chapter 1. The one that started it all.

Dae-hyun’s fingers twitched toward his camera.

Before he could lift it, Park Soo-jin walked in through the front door.

She moved slowly, cane tapping lightly on the wooden floor. Her hanbok coat shimmered in the low light, a deep indigo with silver thread. She wasn’t using the cane for support—more for rhythm, like a metronome set to the tempo of memory.

Min met her at the host stand.

Park Soo-jin stopped. Looked Min up and down. Not with judgment. With something like wonder.

“You kept the apron,” Soo-jin said.

Min touched the scuba-patterned fabric over her chest. “Hadn’t earned a clean one yet.”

Soo-jin almost smiled. Then she glanced past Min, eyes locking onto Dae-hyun.

The air tightened.

Dae-hyun lowered his camera. The silence stretched.

Soo-jin broke it first.

“You,” she said to Dae-hyun. “Still shooting for the devil?”

His jaw tightened. “I’m shooting for the truth now.”

Soo-jin’s laugh was a rustle of silk. “Truth. That’s a heavy word for a man who doctored photos to make a chef look incompetent.”

Dae-hyun didn’t flinch. “And you’re the critic who forgot more than she remembered.”

Soo-jin’s smile faded. She reached into her coat. Pulled out a small flask. Took a quick sip. Capped it. Put it away.

“Memory isn’t the only thing I’m losing,” she said.

Min watched them, arms loose at her sides. No one offered tea. No one poured soju. Just silence and the low hum of the kitchen pulling itself together.

Park Soo-jin set her cane aside. Leaned on the host stand with one hand.

“You’re serving the same dish,” she said to Min. “The one that got you fired. The one you told me about five years ago—the one you said was *yaksik* in motion, not just a stew.”

Min didn’t answer. She didn’t need to.

Soo-jin’s voice dropped. “You’re serving it to me first. Tonight. Before anyone else.”

Min tilted her head. “You want to be the first to taste the wreckage?”

Soo-jin’s grip tightened on the stand. “I want to taste the resurrection.”

Dae-hyun shifted beside Min. His camera was still in his hand, but he wasn’t raising it. He was listening. Watching. Waiting.

Min turned toward the kitchen. “Then you’ll have to wait your turn.”

Soo-jin didn’t move. “I’m not waiting, Min Ji-eun. I’m choosing.”

The line cooks froze. The stove hissed. The sound of a pot lid clattering against a steel surface echoed like a gunshot.

Dae-hyun stepped forward. Blocked the path to the stairs.

“Park Soo-jin,” he said, voice low but firm, “you’re not tasting anything tonight unless you sit where I put you.”

Soo-jin’s eyes flicked to him. “And if I refuse?”

“Then I unplug the livestream,” he said. “And you’re just another drunk critic in a dying neighborhood.”

Soo-jin exhaled through her nose. A slow, measured breath. Then she nodded.

“Fine. Seat me. But I want the corner table by the window. The one with the view of the courtyard.”

Dae-hyun smiled, but it wasn’t warm. “That table’s reserved.”

“For who?”

“For the past,” Dae-hyun said. “You’re welcome to pull up a chair at the present.”

Soo-jin stared at him. Then at Min.

Min stepped between them. “You want the resurrection? Then sit. Eat. And when you’re done, tell the world it’s real.”

Soo-jin’s chin lifted. “And if it’s not?”

Min met her gaze. “Then I’ll close the doors. Burn the recipes. And disappear.”

Soo-jin studied her. Then, with deliberate slowness, she picked up her cane and tapped it once against the floor.

“Then I’ll eat.”

Dae-hyun stepped aside. Let her pass.

Min watched them go up the stairs—Soo-jin first, then Dae-hyun, then Min behind them, her boots quiet on the wood.

At the top of the stairs, the second floor was a single room, eight small tables draped in dark linen. Each set with a single bowl, a pair of black lacquer spoons, and a small ceramic side dish of pickled radish.

The critics were already seated—three of them, murmuring, checking phones. None of them looked up when Min entered.

She moved to the center of the room. The air smelled of toasted barley and aged soybean paste. The broth had been simmering since dawn. She lifted the lid from her bowl. Steam curled into the focused silence.

Park Soo-jin sat at the corner table, her coat spread over the back of her chair. She didn’t reach for the spoon.

Min set the lid down.

“You don’t have to eat it,” Min said.

Soo-jin looked at the bowl. Then at Min.

“I know I don’t,” she said. “I want to.”

Dae-hyun stood near the doorway, camera aimed at the scene. He didn’t press record. Didn’t lower it. Just watched.

Min waited.

Soo-jin finally picked up her spoon. Tapped the rim. Tested the weight.

Then she dipped.

The broth was thick with reduction. A single flake of cod floated to the surface. She lifted it to her lips.

Min didn’t breathe.

Soo-jin’s eyes closed.

The spoon paused halfway to her mouth.

Min’s stomach tightened.

Soo-jin opened her eyes. Looked at Min.

“This isn’t *doenjang-jjigae*,” she said.

Min said nothing.

Soo-jin took the bite.

She didn’t chew right away. Just let the flavors sit on her tongue.

Then she swallowed.

And for the first time in years, Park Soo-jin’s hands stopped shaking.
